name: Categorical Imperative
description: The categorical imperative is Kant's central principle of morality, commanding that we act only according to maxims we could will to become universal laws. Unlike hypothetical imperatives that depend on desired outcomes, the categorical imperative is unconditional—binding on all rational agents regardless of their inclinations. Kant formulated it in several equivalent ways, the most famous being: act only according to that maxim whereby you can at the same time will that it should become a universal law. This grounds morality in pure reason rather than consequences or sentiment.
thinkerBio: Immanuel Kant (1724–1804) was a German philosopher who spent his career at Königsberg; his Groundwork for the Metaphysics of Morals established deontological ethics as a systematic alternative to both consequentialism and divine command theory.
quote: Act only according to that maxim whereby you can at the same time will that it should become a universal law.

books.title: Groundwork of the Metaphysics of Morals
books.author: Immanuel Kant

name: Utilitarianism
description: Utilitarianism holds that the right action is the one that produces the greatest happiness for the greatest number of people. Founded by Jeremy Bentham and refined by John Stuart Mill, it judges actions purely by their consequences—specifically the balance of pleasure over pain they produce. Mill elevated the theory by distinguishing higher and lower pleasures, arguing that intellectual satisfactions are worth more than mere bodily ones. Utilitarianism remains the most influential consequentialist framework in contemporary ethical and political debate.
thinkerBio: Jeremy Bentham (1748–1832) founded utilitarianism and proposed the hedonic calculus; John Stuart Mill (1806–1873) refined it by distinguishing higher from lower pleasures and connecting it to liberal political philosophy in On Liberty and Utilitarianism.
quote: It is better to be Socrates dissatisfied than a fool satisfied.

name: Virtue Ethics
description: Virtue ethics focuses on the character of the moral agent rather than on rules or consequences. Aristotle argued that a good life is one of eudaimonia—flourishing—achieved by cultivating virtues like courage, justice, and practical wisdom. Virtues are stable dispositions to act, feel, and reason in excellent ways, developed through habit and practice. The virtuous person does the right thing, at the right time, toward the right person, in the right way—not from duty or calculation but from a well-formed character.
thinkerBio: Aristotle (384–322 BCE) was a student of Plato whose Nicomachean Ethics remains the foundational text of virtue theory; the approach was revived in analytic philosophy by G.E.M. Anscombe, Philippa Foot, and Alasdair MacIntyre from the 1950s onward.
quote: We are what we repeatedly do. Excellence, then, is not an act but a habit.

name: Falsifiability
description: Falsifiability is Karl Popper's criterion for distinguishing genuine science from pseudoscience: a theory is scientific only if it makes predictions that could, in principle, be proven wrong by observation or experiment. Popper argued that science does not progress by accumulating verified instances but by bold conjectures subjected to rigorous attempts at refutation. A theory that explains everything—and therefore can never be falsified—explains nothing scientifically. This demarcation criterion has profoundly shaped both the philosophy of science and scientific practice itself.
thinkerBio: Karl Popper (1902–1994) was an Austrian-British philosopher of science who developed falsifiability in The Logic of Scientific Discovery (1934/1959); he also made major contributions to political philosophy in The Open Society and Its Enemies (1945).
quote: A theory that is not refutable by any conceivable event is non-scientific.

books.title: Criticism and the Growth of Knowledge
books.author: Lakatos and Musgrave (eds.)

name: Paradigm Shift
description: Thomas Kuhn's concept of the paradigm shift describes how scientific revolutions occur not through gradual accumulation but through sudden, discontinuous overturning of entire frameworks. Normal science operates within an accepted paradigm—a set of assumptions, methods, and exemplary problems—until anomalies accumulate and trigger a crisis. A scientific revolution replaces one paradigm with another that is incommensurable with its predecessor, meaning the two cannot be directly compared on the same rational scale. Kuhn's account challenged the view of science as purely rational and progressive.
thinkerBio: Thomas Kuhn (1922–1996) was an American historian and philosopher of science whose The Structure of Scientific Revolutions (1962) is one of the most cited academic books of the twentieth century; he taught at MIT and Princeton.
quote: Normal science does not aim at novelties of fact or theory and, when successful, finds none.

books.title: Criticism and the Growth of Knowledge
books.author: Lakatos and Musgrave (eds.)

name: Existentialism
description: Existentialism holds that human beings are radically free and therefore fully responsible for who they are and what they do. Sartre's slogan 'existence precedes essence' captures the core claim: we are not born with a fixed human nature or purpose but must create ourselves through our choices. This freedom is inescapable and constitutes both our dignity and our burden—Sartre called the anxiety it generates 'anguish.' Existentialism emerged partly as a response to the apparent meaninglessness revealed by twentieth-century catastrophe and influenced literature, psychology, and political thought.
thinkerBio: Jean-Paul Sartre (1905–1980) was a French philosopher, playwright, and novelist who declined the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1964. His philosophical works, including Being and Nothingness, and his plays and novels made existentialism a major cultural movement in post-war Europe.
quote: Man first of all exists, encounters himself, surges up in the world—and defines himself afterwards.

name: Empiricism
description: Empiricism is the view that all knowledge derives from sensory experience. Against the rationalist claim that some knowledge is innate or available through reason alone, Locke argued that the mind begins as a blank slate—a tabula rasa—filled entirely by experience. Hume extended this by arguing that even our ideas of causation and the self are nothing more than patterns of sensation. Empiricism underpins modern science and remains the dominant epistemological framework among scientists and many philosophers today.
thinkerBio: John Locke (1632–1704) was an English philosopher and physician whose work on epistemology and political theory profoundly shaped the Enlightenment. His Essay Concerning Human Understanding argues that all knowledge originates in sense experience, while his political writings influenced the American Declaration of Independence.
quote: The senses at first let in particular ideas, and furnish the yet empty cabinet.

name: Social Contract
description: The social contract is the idea that political authority is legitimate only when grounded in an agreement—implicit or explicit—among the people it governs. Hobbes saw the contract as a surrender of freedoms to a sovereign in exchange for security. Locke grounded it in natural rights and made government answerable to the governed. Rousseau added the concept of the 'general will'—the collective good that legitimate government must express. Social contract theory remains the dominant framework for liberal democratic legitimacy.
thinkerBio: Thomas Hobbes (1588–1679) grounded the contract in fear of violent death; John Locke (1632–1704) grounded it in natural rights; Jean-Jacques Rousseau (1712–1778) grounded it in the general will — together they represent the three major versions of social contract theory.
quote: Man is born free, and everywhere he is in chains.

name: Hard Problem of Consciousness
description: The hard problem of consciousness, named by David Chalmers, is the challenge of explaining why physical processes in the brain give rise to subjective experience—why there is 'something it is like' to see red or feel pain. The easy problems, by contrast, concern how the brain processes information, integrates stimuli, and controls behavior—difficult but tractable scientifically. The hard problem may require entirely new concepts or even a revision of our understanding of nature, as no purely functional explanation seems to account for the felt quality of experience.
thinkerBio: David Chalmers (b. 1966) at NYU articulated the hard problem in 'Facing Up to the Problem of Consciousness' (1995) and The Conscious Mind (1996); Daniel Dennett (b. 1942) at Tufts has been his principal opponent in the most productive debate in contemporary philosophy of mind.
quote: Why is there something it is like to undergo conscious experience? Why doesn't all this information-processing go on in the dark?

name: Language Games
description: Wittgenstein's concept of language games captures the idea that words get their meaning from their use within specific social practices and forms of life, not from private mental meanings or reference to abstract objects. A 'language game' is any rule-governed activity in which language plays a role—from giving orders to telling jokes to scientific inquiry. There is no single essence to language, just a family of overlapping practices. This view dismantled the picture theory of meaning he had earlier proposed and redirected philosophy toward attention to ordinary usage.
thinkerBio: Ludwig Wittgenstein (1889–1951) was an Austrian-British philosopher whose Tractatus Logico-Philosophicus (1921) and Philosophical Investigations (1953) represent two radically different philosophies; the Investigations, published posthumously, introduced language games as a therapeutic tool.
quote: The meaning of a word is its use in the language.

name: Moral Relativism
thinkerBio: Moral relativism draws on anthropologists like Ruth Benedict (1887–1948) and philosophers including Gilbert Harman (1938–2021); its modern philosophical articulation owes most to Harman's work, though the empirical motivation comes from cross-cultural anthropology.
quote: What is right in one culture may be wrong in another, and neither verdict is objectively correct.

name: Original Position
thinkerBio: John Rawls (1921–2002) was a Harvard political philosopher whose A Theory of Justice (1971) revived systematic political philosophy after decades of metaethical preoccupation; his work dominated political philosophy for the final decades of the twentieth century.
quote: Justice is the first virtue of social institutions, as truth is of systems of thought.

books.title: Liberalism and the Limits of Justice
books.author: Michael Sandel

books.title: Justice: What's the Right Thing to Do?
books.author: Michael Sandel

name: Care Ethics
thinkerBio: Carol Gilligan (b. 1936) challenged Kohlberg's male-biased moral development theory in In a Different Voice (1982); Nel Noddings (b. 1929) and Virginia Held (b. 1929) developed care ethics into a systematic philosophical framework over subsequent decades.
quote: The ideal of care is an activity of relationship, of seeing and responding to need, taking care of the world by sustaining the web of connection.

books.title: Caring: A Feminine Approach to Ethics
books.author: Nel Noddings

name: Moral Luck
thinkerBio: Thomas Nagel (b. 1937) at NYU and Bernard Williams (1929–2003) jointly introduced the concept in a 1976 symposium; their contrasting approaches — Nagel more systematic, Williams more skeptical of moral theory — define the two main positions in the debate.
quote: The truck driver who accidentally kills a child is not merely unlucky — his life is changed, and we judge him differently.

books.title: Ethics and the Limits of Philosophy
books.author: Bernard Williams

name: Contractualism
thinkerBio: John Rawls (1921–2002) developed institutional contractualism in A Theory of Justice; T.M. Scanlon (b. 1940) at Harvard developed a distinct account of individual moral wrongness in What We Owe to Each Other (1998), making the tradition two-layered.
quote: An act is wrong if its performance would be disallowed by any set of principles that no one could reasonably reject as a basis for general agreement.

books.title: What We Owe to Each Other
books.author: T.M. Scanlon

name: Eudaimonia
thinkerBio: Aristotle (384–322 BCE) developed eudaimonia in the Nicomachean Ethics as the ultimate human good; Martha Nussbaum (b. 1947) at the University of Chicago produced the most influential contemporary development through the capabilities approach.
quote: Happiness is the meaning and the purpose of life, the whole aim and end of human existence.

name: The Trolley Problem
thinkerBio: Philippa Foot (1920–2010) introduced the original trolley scenario in 1967 in her paper on the doctrine of double effect; Judith Jarvis Thomson (b. 1929) at MIT developed the footbridge variant and systematized the doing/allowing distinction the cases illuminate.
quote: It seems right to turn the trolley and save five lives, even though this means killing one person.

books.title: Would You Kill the Fat Man?
books.author: David Edmonds

name: Substance Dualism
thinkerBio: René Descartes (1596–1650) was a French philosopher and mathematician who inaugurated modern philosophy through radical doubt; his Meditations on First Philosophy (1641) remains the canonical statement of the mind-body problem.
quote: I think, therefore I am.

name: Possible Worlds
thinkerBio: Saul Kripke (b. 1940) introduced possible worlds semantics for modal logic in the late 1950s; David Lewis (1941–2001) at Princeton developed modal realism — the radical claim that possible worlds are literally real concrete universes — in On the Plurality of Worlds (1986).
quote: I believe, and so do you, that things could have been different in countless ways.

name: The Ship of Theseus
thinkerBio: The puzzle originates with Plutarch (c. 46–120 CE) in his Life of Theseus; its most philosophically rigorous modern treatment is Derek Parfit's (1942–2017) application to personal identity in Reasons and Persons (1984).
quote: If every plank has been replaced, is it still the same ship?

name: Causation
thinkerBio: David Hume (1711–1776) was a Scottish philosopher whose analysis of causation in the Treatise of Human Nature (1739) and Enquiry Concerning Human Understanding (1748) remains the unavoidable starting point for all subsequent theories of causation.
quote: We have no other notion of cause and effect but that of certain objects which have always been conjoined together.

name: Eternal Recurrence
thinkerBio: Friedrich Nietzsche (1844–1900) was a German philosopher and philologist who published prolifically from 1872 until his mental collapse in 1889; eternal recurrence appears first in The Gay Science (1882) and is developed most fully in Thus Spoke Zarathustra.
quote: What, if some day or night a demon were to steal after you into your loneliest loneliness and say to you: 'This life as you now live it, you will have to live once more and innumerable times more'?

name: Panpsychism
thinkerBio: David Chalmers (b. 1966) at NYU articulated the hard problem that motivates panpsychism; Philip Goff (b. 1981) at Durham has written the most accessible contemporary defence in Galileo's Error (2019).
quote: Consciousness is a fundamental feature of the world, alongside mass, charge, and spin.

name: Nihilism
thinkerBio: Friedrich Nietzsche (1844–1900) provided nihilism's definitive philosophical diagnosis; the term was popularized in Ivan Turgenev's Fathers and Sons (1862) but its philosophical depth comes from Nietzsche's analysis in The Gay Science, The Will to Power, and Thus Spoke Zarathustra.
quote: God is dead. God remains dead. And we have killed him.

name: Essentialism
thinkerBio: Aristotle (384–322 BCE) developed essentialism in the Metaphysics and biological works; Saul Kripke (b. 1940) revived it through possible worlds semantics in Naming and Necessity (1972/1980), demonstrating that essential truths are necessary but discovered empirically.
quote: Water is necessarily H₂O — this was not known a priori, but once discovered, it is necessarily true.

name: Process Philosophy
thinkerBio: Alfred North Whitehead (1861–1947) was a British mathematician who developed process metaphysics in Process and Reality (1929) after collaborating with Russell on Principia Mathematica; he taught at Harvard from 1924 until his death.
quote: The art of progress is to preserve order amid change, and to preserve change amid order.

name: Identity Over Time
thinkerBio: John Locke (1632–1704) formulated the memory criterion in An Essay Concerning Human Understanding (1689); Derek Parfit (1942–2017) transformed the debate in Reasons and Persons (1984) by arguing that personal identity is indeterminate and not what matters in survival.
quote: Personal identity consists not in the identity of substance, but in the identity of consciousness.

name: Cogito Ergo Sum
thinkerBio: René Descartes (1596–1650) first stated the cogito in Discourse on the Method (1637) and developed it fully in Meditations on First Philosophy (1641); his method of radical doubt and the certainty found in self-consciousness inaugurated modern epistemology.
quote: I think, therefore I am.

name: The Gettier Problem
thinkerBio: Edmund Gettier (b. 1927) published his three-page paper 'Is Justified True Belief Knowledge?' in Analysis in 1963, launching one of the most productive research programs in analytic epistemology; he taught at the University of Massachusetts Amherst for his entire career.
quote: It is possible for a person to have justified true belief and yet fail to have knowledge.

name: Pragmatism
thinkerBio: Charles Sanders Peirce (1839–1914) introduced the pragmatic maxim; William James (1842–1910) extended it to truth; John Dewey (1859–1952) developed it into a comprehensive philosophy of inquiry, education, and democracy — together they constitute classical American pragmatism.
quote: The true is only the expedient in the way of our thinking, just as the right is only the expedient in the way of our behaving.

name: Epistemic Injustice
thinkerBio: Miranda Fricker (b. 1966) is a British philosopher at CUNY Graduate Center whose Epistemic Injustice: Power and the Ethics of Knowing (2007) created a new subfield at the intersection of epistemology, feminist philosophy, and political philosophy.
quote: Epistemic injustice is a wrong done to someone specifically in their capacity as a knower.

books.title: The Routledge Handbook of Epistemic Injustice
books.author: Kidd, Medina, Pohlhaus (eds.)

name: A Priori / A Posteriori
thinkerBio: Immanuel Kant (1724–1804) gave the distinction its modern philosophical form in the Critique of Pure Reason (1781); Saul Kripke's Naming and Necessity (1972) showed that necessity and a priority come apart — one of the most important results in twentieth-century analytic philosophy.
quote: Thoughts without content are empty, intuitions without concepts are blind.

name: Coherentism
thinkerBio: Otto Neurath (1882–1945) introduced the famous ship metaphor for coherentist justification; Laurence BonJour (b. 1943) at the University of Washington developed the most rigorous contemporary coherentist epistemology in The Structure of Empirical Knowledge (1985).
quote: Like sailors who must rebuild their ship on the open sea, we reconstruct our knowledge piece by piece while remaining afloat.

name: Skepticism
thinkerBio: Pyrrho of Elis (c. 360–270 BCE) founded ancient skepticism; René Descartes (1596–1650) developed modern skepticism as a method; David Hume (1711–1776) made the most trenchant modern case for its inescapability in matters of induction and causation.
quote: If you would be a real seeker after truth, it is necessary that at least once in your life you doubt, as far as possible, all things.

books.title: Skepticism and the Veil of Perception
books.author: Michael Huemer

name: Existence Precedes Essence
thinkerBio: Jean-Paul Sartre (1905–1980) was a French philosopher, novelist, and playwright who became the leading figure of existentialism; Being and Nothingness (1943) developed the full philosophical account of radical freedom that the slogan 'existence precedes essence' encapsulates.
quote: Man is condemned to be free.

name: The Absurd
thinkerBio: Albert Camus (1913–1960) was an Algerian-French novelist and philosopher who developed the absurd in The Myth of Sisyphus (1942) and extended it politically in The Rebel (1951); he won the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1957 and died in a car accident at 46.
quote: There is but one truly serious philosophical problem, and that is suicide.

name: Bad Faith
thinkerBio: Jean-Paul Sartre (1905–1980) developed bad faith (mauvaise foi) as the central concept of existentialist moral psychology in Being and Nothingness (1943) — the self-deception by which consciousness denies its own radical freedom and responsibility.
quote: Man is always more than what he is said to be — bad faith is the attempt to fix oneself as a thing.

name: Being-toward-death
thinkerBio: Martin Heidegger (1889–1976) was a German philosopher whose Being and Time (1927) is one of the most influential and difficult texts in twentieth-century philosophy; his analysis of being-toward-death transformed how philosophy thinks about finitude and authentic existence.
quote: Death is Dasein's ownmost possibility — non-relational, certain, and indefinite as to its When.

name: Thrownness
thinkerBio: Martin Heidegger (1889–1976) developed thrownness (Geworfenheit) as part of the existential analytic of Dasein in Being and Time (1927) — the fundamental condition of finding oneself already in a situation one did not choose.
quote: Dasein finds itself thrown into existence — already in a world it did not author, with a past it did not choose.

name: Will to Power
thinkerBio: Friedrich Nietzsche (1844–1900) developed the will to power across Thus Spoke Zarathustra (1883–85), Beyond Good and Evil (1886), and On the Genealogy of Morality (1887); the concept was distorted by his sister Elisabeth for Nazi appropriation after his 1889 mental collapse.
quote: The will to power is the unexhausted procreative will of life.

name: Angst
thinkerBio: Søren Kierkegaard (1813–1855) analyzed anxiety as the dizziness of freedom in The Concept of Anxiety (1844); Martin Heidegger (1889–1976) transformed it into an ontological concept in Being and Time (1927) — both texts are foundational for the existentialist tradition.
quote: Anxiety is the dizziness of freedom.

name: Authenticity
thinkerBio: Martin Heidegger (1889–1976) developed authenticity (Eigentlichkeit) as an existential structural concept in Being and Time (1927); Jean-Paul Sartre (1905–1980) gave it more concrete ethical content through the contrast with bad faith and the demands of radical freedom.
quote: Authenticity means taking over one's own existence as genuinely one's own — no longer living at the pace of what one does.

name: Deconstruction
thinkerBio: Jacques Derrida (1930–2004) was an Algerian-French philosopher whose Of Grammatology (1967), Writing and Difference (1967), and Speech and Phenomena (1967) introduced deconstruction and transformed literary theory, legal studies, and philosophy of language.
quote: There is nothing outside the text.

name: Speech Act Theory
thinkerBio: J.L. Austin (1911–1960) developed speech act theory at Oxford in How to Do Things with Words (1962, posthumous); John Searle (b. 1932) at Berkeley systematized and extended the theory, connecting it to intentionality, social ontology, and the nature of consciousness.
quote: To say something is to do something.

books.title: How to Do Things with Words
books.author: J.L. Austin

name: The Liar's Paradox
thinkerBio: The paradox is attributed to Epimenides the Cretan (sixth century BCE); Alfred Tarski (1901–1983) provided the most influential formal response through the hierarchy of metalanguages in 'The Concept of Truth in Formalized Languages' (1933).
quote: This sentence is false.

books.title: Truth and the Absence of Fact
books.author: Hartry Field

name: Structuralism
thinkerBio: Ferdinand de Saussure (1857–1913) established structural linguistics in Course in General Linguistics (1916); Claude Lévi-Strauss (1908–2009) applied the structural method to anthropology — together they define the structuralist movement that transformed the humanities.
quote: In language there are only differences, with no positive terms.

name: Hermeneutics
thinkerBio: Wilhelm Dilthey (1833–1911) established hermeneutics as a philosophical discipline; Hans-Georg Gadamer (1900–2002) transformed it in Truth and Method (1960); Paul Ricoeur (1913–2005) extended it to narrative, symbol, and action theory.
quote: To understand is always to understand otherwise.

name: Occam's Razor
thinkerBio: William of Ockham (c. 1287–1347) was an English Franciscan friar and philosopher who wielded parsimony extensively against Scholastic metaphysics; the 'razor' bears his name though it was formulated verbatim by later commentators from his principle 'Plurality must never be posited without necessity.'
quote: Do not multiply entities beyond necessity.

books.title: The Philosophy of Science: An Introduction
books.author: Samir Okasha

name: Dialectics
thinkerBio: Georg Wilhelm Friedrich Hegel (1770–1831) developed dialectics as the inner logic of Geist in the Phenomenology of Spirit (1807) and the Science of Logic (1812–16); Karl Marx (1818–1883) inverted Hegel's idealist dialectic into a materialist one grounded in class conflict.
quote: The truth is the whole.

name: Alienation
thinkerBio: G.W.F. Hegel (1770–1831) introduced alienation as a stage in Spirit's self-development; Karl Marx (1818–1883) transformed it into a critique of capitalism in the Economic and Philosophic Manuscripts (1844) and implicitly throughout Capital.
quote: The worker becomes poorer the more wealth he produces.

name: Hegemony
thinkerBio: Antonio Gramsci (1891–1937) was an Italian Marxist theorist who developed hegemony while imprisoned by Mussolini; his Prison Notebooks (written 1929–1935, published 1947–51) constitute one of the most original contributions to twentieth-century political philosophy.
quote: The dominant class does not merely rule — it leads. Hegemony is leadership by consent rather than domination by force.

name: Civil Disobedience
thinkerBio: Henry David Thoreau (1817–1862) coined the term in 'Resistance to Civil Government' (1849); Mahatma Gandhi (1869–1948) and Martin Luther King Jr. (1929–1968) transformed it into a mass political practice that changed history in India and the American South.
quote: Under a government which imprisons any unjustly, the true place for a just man is also a prison.

books.title: On the Duty of Civil Disobedience
books.author: Henry David Thoreau

name: Biopower
thinkerBio: Michel Foucault (1926–1984) was a French philosopher and historian of ideas whose Discipline and Punish (1975) and The History of Sexuality Vol. 1 (1976) developed the concept of biopower; his Collège de France lectures elaborated its biopolitical dimensions.
quote: The old power of death now gives way to the administration of bodies and the calculated management of life.

name: Democracy
thinkerBio: Jean-Jacques Rousseau (1712–1778) grounded democracy in the general will; John Dewey (1859–1952) argued democracy is primarily a form of associated life; Jürgen Habermas (b. 1929) developed deliberative democracy grounded in communicative rationality.
quote: Democracy is not just a form of government — it is primarily a mode of associated living.

name: Republicanism
thinkerBio: Philip Pettit (b. 1945) at Princeton reconstructed the republican tradition in Republicanism (1997); Hannah Arendt (1906–1975) developed a contrasting republican emphasis on political action and the public realm in The Human Condition (1958).
quote: Freedom is not the absence of interference — it is the absence of arbitrary power over one's life.

books.title: Domination and the Arts of Resistance
books.author: James Scott

name: Liberalism
thinkerBio: John Locke (1632–1704) grounded natural rights liberalism in self-ownership; John Stuart Mill (1806–1873) grounded it in the harm principle; John Rawls (1921–2002) constructed a systematic liberal theory from a contractualist foundation — together they define the liberal tradition.
quote: The only purpose for which power can be rightfully exercised over any member of a civilised community, against his will, is to prevent harm to others.

name: Qualia
thinkerBio: Thomas Nagel (b. 1937) at NYU introduced the what-it's-like formulation in 'What Is It Like to Be a Bat?' (1974); Frank Jackson (b. 1943) developed the knowledge argument through Mary's room in 'Epiphenomenal Qualia' (1982) — Jackson later recanted but the argument remains foundational.
quote: What it is like to see red — the redness of red — is not captured by any physical description of the visual system.

books.title: Philosophy of Mind: Classical and Contemporary Readings
books.author: David Chalmers (ed.)

name: Chinese Room
thinkerBio: John Searle (b. 1932) at Berkeley published 'Minds, Brains, and Programs' in Behavioral and Brain Sciences in 1980 with over 20 peer commentaries; the Chinese Room remains the most discussed thought experiment in philosophy of mind and philosophy of AI.
quote: Syntax is not sufficient for semantics — symbol manipulation is not understanding.

name: Functionalism
thinkerBio: Hilary Putnam (1926–2016) at Harvard introduced multiple realizability and functionalism in the 1960s–70s; Jerry Fodor (1935–2017) developed the language of thought as the functionalist cognitive architecture — both defined the computational theory of mind for a generation.
quote: Mental states are defined by their causal roles, not by their physical composition.

name: Free Will vs Determinism
thinkerBio: The modern debate was shaped by David Hume (1711–1776) and Immanuel Kant (1724–1804); Peter Strawson (1919–2006) transformed it with 'Freedom and Resentment' (1962); Peter van Inwagen (b. 1942) and Harry Frankfurt (b. 1929) define the contemporary incompatibilist and compatibilist positions.
quote: The question of free will is whether our choices can be genuinely ours in a causally determined world.

name: Personal Identity
thinkerBio: John Locke (1632–1704) formulated the memory criterion in An Essay Concerning Human Understanding (1689); Derek Parfit (1942–2017) transformed the debate in Reasons and Persons (1984) by arguing that personal identity is indeterminate and not what matters in survival.
quote: Personal identity consists not in the identity of substance, but in the identity of consciousness.

name: Embodied Cognition
thinkerBio: Maurice Merleau-Ponty (1908–1961) provided the phenomenological foundation in Phenomenology of Perception (1945); Francisco Varela (1946–2001), Evan Thompson, and Eleanor Rosch developed enactivist cognitive science in The Embodied Mind (1991).
quote: The body is not an object the mind uses — it is the very medium through which we have a world.

name: Dharma
thinkerBio: The concept of dharma is central to all Indian philosophical and religious traditions; it is discussed systematically in the Dharmasutras, the Bhagavad Gita, and in Buddhist texts including the Dhammapada and the Pali Canon, representing over two millennia of philosophical development.
quote: Better is one's own dharma, though imperfectly performed, than the dharma of another well performed.

name: Karma
thinkerBio: The concept of karma is found across Hindu, Buddhist, and Jain traditions; its first systematic discussion appears in the Upanishads (c. 800–400 BCE), with the Bhagavad Gita providing the most philosophically sophisticated treatment through the doctrine of nishkama karma.
quote: As a man acts, as he behaves, so does he become — the doer of good becomes good, the doer of evil becomes evil.

books.title: Karma and Rebirth in Classical Indian Traditions
books.author: Wendy Doniger O'Flaherty (ed.)

name: Wu Wei
thinkerBio: Wu wei is the central practical virtue of Taoism, developed by Laozi (sixth/fifth century BCE) in the Tao Te Ching and Zhuangzi (c. 369–286 BCE) in the Zhuangzi — both texts remain the primary philosophical sources for the doctrine of non-action.
quote: The Tao does nothing, yet leaves nothing undone.

name: Anatta
thinkerBio: The doctrine of no-self (anatta/anatman) was first taught by the historical Buddha (c. 480–400 BCE) in the Anattalakkhana Sutta; it was developed philosophically by the Abhidharma schools and most rigorously by Nagarjuna in the Madhyamaka tradition.
quote: When searching for the self, you find only processes — there is no separate, permanent self to be found.

books.title: The Heart of the Buddha's Teaching
books.author: Thich Nhat Hanh

name: Advaita Vedanta
thinkerBio: Adi Shankaracharya (c. 788–820 CE) was an Indian philosopher and theologian who systematized Advaita Vedanta in commentaries on the Upanishads, the Bhagavad Gita, and the Brahma Sutras; he revitalized Hinduism and established four monastic centers across India.
quote: Brahman alone is real, the world is appearance, and the individual self is non-different from Brahman.

books.title: A History of Indian Philosophy Vol. 1
books.author: Surendranath Dasgupta

name: Dukkha
thinkerBio: The Buddha (c. 563–483 BCE) declared dukkha the first of the Four Noble Truths in his first sermon; the entire Buddhist philosophical and soteriological project flows from this diagnosis — understanding dukkha is the beginning of the path to liberation.
quote: Birth is dukkha, aging is dukkha, sickness is dukkha, death is dukkha — the five aggregates of clinging are dukkha.

name: Zen Koans
thinkerBio: The koan tradition was systematized by Dahui Zonggao (1089–1163 CE) in China and developed in Japan through the Rinzai school founded by Eisai (1141–1215 CE); the most famous collection, the Mumonkan (Gateless Barrier), was compiled by Wumen Huikai (1183–1260 CE).
quote: What is the sound of one hand clapping?

name: Confucian Ren
thinkerBio: Confucius (551–479 BCE) is the Latinized name of Kong Qiu, whose conversations recorded in the Analects became the foundation of Confucianism; Mencius (371–289 BCE) further developed ren in the Mengzi, grounding it in the innate moral sprouts of human nature.
quote: The man of ren, wishing to be established himself, seeks also to establish others; wishing to be enlarged himself, he seeks also to enlarge others.

name: The Sublime
thinkerBio: Edmund Burke (1729–1797) wrote A Philosophical Enquiry into the Origin of Our Ideas of the Sublime and Beautiful (1757) — the first systematic aesthetic treatment; Immanuel Kant (1724–1804) gave it philosophical rigor in the Critique of Judgment (1790), making it central to Romantic aesthetics.
quote: Whatever is fitted in any sort to excite the ideas of pain and danger — whatever is in any sort terrible — is a source of the sublime.

books.title: A Philosophical Enquiry into the Sublime and Beautiful
books.author: Edmund Burke

name: Mimesis
thinkerBio: Plato (428–348 BCE) attacked mimesis in the Republic as epistemically and morally dangerous; Aristotle (384–322 BCE) defended it in the Poetics as philosophically valuable — the imitation of universal human patterns rather than mere copying of particulars.
quote: The object of mimesis is not what has happened, but what might happen according to necessity or probability.

name: Aesthetic Disinterestedness
thinkerBio: Immanuel Kant (1724–1804) formulated aesthetic disinterestedness in the Critique of Judgment (1790) as the defining feature of genuinely aesthetic pleasure; Arthur Schopenhauer (1788–1860) radicalized the concept in The World as Will and Representation (1818).
quote: Taste is the faculty of estimating an object or a mode of representation by means of a delight or aversion apart from any interest.

books.title: The World as Will and Representation Vol. 1
books.author: Arthur Schopenhauer

name: Katharsis
thinkerBio: Aristotle (384–322 BCE) introduced katharsis in the Poetics (c. 335 BCE) in a single famously obscure sentence; the debate about its meaning — purgation, purification, or clarification — has continued for two thousand years.
quote: Tragedy effects through pity and fear the proper purgation of these emotions.

books.title: Aristotle on the Art of Poetry
books.author: Ingram Bywater

name: Art as Expression
thinkerBio: Benedetto Croce (1866–1952) identified art with intuitive expression in Aesthetic (1902); R.G. Collingwood (1889–1943) refined this in The Principles of Art (1938), arguing that genuine art is the process of clarifying emotional experience rather than producing a finished artifact.
quote: Art is the expression of emotion, and expression is a process of clarification — not the discharge of feeling but its transformation into something understood.

name: The Uncanny
thinkerBio: Sigmund Freud (1856–1939) published 'The Uncanny' (Das Unheimliche) in 1919, drawing on Ernst Jentsch's earlier work; Martin Heidegger (1889–1976) used Unheimlichkeit independently in Being and Time (1927) as an ontological concept.
quote: The uncanny is that class of the frightening which leads back to what is known of old and long familiar.

name: Art and Morality
thinkerBio: Plato (428–348 BCE) made the debate unavoidable by expelling poets from the Republic; Aristotle responded with the Poetics; the contemporary debate is shaped by Berys Gaut's ethicism, Noël Carroll's moderate moralism, and Daniel Jacobson's autonomism.
quote: We should not allow the soul to become angry and sorrowful — the arts must be censored to protect virtue.

name: Scientific Realism
thinkerBio: Hilary Putnam (1926–2016) formulated the no-miracles argument for scientific realism; Richard Boyd (b. 1942) developed a sophisticated causal scientific realism; Bas van Fraassen (b. 1941) at Princeton produced the most rigorous contemporary anti-realist alternative in The Scientific Image (1980).
quote: The only philosophy that doesn't make the success of science a miracle is realism.

name: Underdetermination
thinkerBio: Pierre Duhem (1861–1916) identified holistic testing in physics in The Aim and Structure of Physical Theory (1906); W.V.O. Quine (1908–2000) generalized it in 'Two Dogmas of Empiricism' (1951) — one of the most influential papers in twentieth-century philosophy.
quote: Our statements about the external world face the tribunal of sense experience not individually but only as a corporate body.

books.title: The Aim and Structure of Physical Theory
books.author: Pierre Duhem

books.title: From a Logical Point of View
books.author: W.V.O. Quine

name: Theory-Ladenness of Observation
thinkerBio: N.R. Hanson (1924–1967) made the philosophical case in Patterns of Discovery (1958); Thomas Kuhn (1922–1996) developed it into his thesis about paradigm incommensurability; Paul Feyerabend (1924–1994) drew the most radical conclusions in Against Method (1975).
quote: There is more to seeing than meets the eyeball.

name: Inference to Best Explanation
thinkerBio: Charles Sanders Peirce (1839–1914) introduced abduction as a third form of inference; Peter Lipton (1954–2007) at Cambridge gave IBE its most sophisticated philosophical treatment in Inference to the Best Explanation (1991/2004).
quote: We infer the truth of a hypothesis because it provides the best explanation of the evidence.

name: Scientific Explanation
thinkerBio: Carl Hempel (1905–1997) developed the deductive-nomological model in 'Studies in the Logic of Explanation' (1948) with Paul Oppenheim; Wesley Salmon (1925–2001) at Pittsburgh proposed the causal-mechanical alternative in Scientific Explanation and the Causal Structure of the World (1984).
quote: To explain a phenomenon is to subsume it under a general law — to show that it had to happen given the conditions and the laws.

books.title: Scientific Explanation and the Causal Structure of the World
books.author: Wesley Salmon

name: Philosophy of Biology
thinkerBio: Ernst Mayr (1904–2005) was a German-American biologist and philosopher whose work on species concepts and teleology shaped the field; Elliott Sober (b. 1948) at Wisconsin has been the dominant philosopher of biology for four decades, connecting formal evolutionary theory to philosophy of causation.
quote: The species problem is the most important and most difficult problem in taxonomic biology.

name: Ubuntu
thinkerBio: Ubuntu is a pan-African philosophical concept rooted in Nguni Bantu cultures; contemporary philosophers who have developed it academically include Thaddeus Metz, Mogobe Ramose, and Desmond Tutu; it provided the philosophical basis for the South African Truth and Reconciliation Commission.
quote: Umuntu ngumuntu ngabantu — a person is a person through other persons.

books.title: Ubuntu: An African Philosophy of Ethics
books.author: Mogobe Ramose

name: Negritude
thinkerBio: Aimé Césaire (1913–2008) was a Martinican poet and politician; Léopold Sédar Senghor (1906–2001) was a Senegalese poet and the first president of Senegal — both co-founded Negritude in 1930s Paris as a response to French assimilationist colonial policy.
quote: My Negritude is not a stone, its deafness hurled against the clamor of the day — it dips into the red flesh of the soil.

books.title: Notebook of a Return to the Native Land
books.author: Aimé Césaire

name: African Communalism
thinkerBio: Kwame Gyekye (1939–2019) was a Ghanaian philosopher who developed moderate communitarianism in Tradition and Modernity (1997); Kwasi Wiredu (1931–2022) at the University of South Florida developed consensus democracy and conceptual decolonization throughout his career.
quote: The good person is one who has a friendly, generous attitude toward others — who is constituted by and through community.

books.title: The Moral Foundation of Communitarian Africa
books.author: Thaddeus Metz

name: Pan-Africanism
thinkerBio: W.E.B. Du Bois (1868–1963) organized the first Pan-African Congress in 1919 and articulated double consciousness; Marcus Garvey (1887–1940) founded the Universal Negro Improvement Association and developed a separatist Pan-Africanism focused on economic self-sufficiency.
quote: The problem of the twentieth century is the problem of the color line.

books.title: The Philosophy and Opinions of Marcus Garvey
books.author: Marcus Garvey

name: Sage Philosophy
thinkerBio: Henry Odera Oruka (1944–1995) was a Kenyan philosopher at the University of Nairobi who developed sage philosophy as a methodology for recovering African individual philosophical thought; his Sage Philosophy (1990) demonstrated individual critical reflection in traditional sages outside academic institutions.
quote: African sages philosophize on the nature of reality, on morality, on God and the good life — not as collective belief but as individual critical reflection.

name: The Second Sex
thinkerBio: Simone de Beauvoir (1908–1986) was a French existentialist philosopher, novelist, and feminist activist; The Second Sex (1949) was her most philosophically ambitious work — applying Sartrean existentialism to the condition of women and founding modern feminist philosophy.
quote: One is not born, but rather becomes, a woman.

name: Intersectionality
thinkerBio: Kimberlé Crenshaw (b. 1959) is an American legal scholar who introduced intersectionality in 'Demarginalizing the Intersection of Race and Sex' (1989) and 'Mapping the Margins' (1991); she developed it specifically to address the failures of anti-discrimination law to protect Black women.
quote: If I were to identify a single metaphor for intersectionality, it would be a traffic intersection — multiple roads of oppression crossing simultaneously.

name: Gender Performativity
thinkerBio: Judith Butler (b. 1956) is an American philosopher at Berkeley whose Gender Trouble (1990) transformed feminist theory, queer theory, and philosophy of gender; drawing on Foucault, Derrida, and Lacan, Butler argued that gender is constituted through repeated performance within regulatory frameworks.
quote: There is no gender identity behind the expressions of gender — that identity is performatively constituted by the very expressions that are said to be its results.

name: Standpoint Epistemology
thinkerBio: Nancy Hartsock (1943–2015) developed standpoint epistemology from a Marxist feminist framework in 'The Feminist Standpoint' (1983); Sandra Harding (b. 1935) extended it into a comprehensive feminist philosophy of science in The Science Question in Feminism (1986).
quote: The standpoint of the oppressed provides not merely a different perspective but an epistemically superior vantage point on certain features of social reality.

name: Supererogation
thinkerBio: J.O. Urmson (1915–2012) introduced the concept in a 1958 paper showing that standard moral theories lack a category for saintly or heroic acts beyond duty; Joel Feinberg (1926–2004) and David Heyd subsequently developed its systematic philosophical analysis.
quote: Some acts go beyond what duty requires — they are not obligatory, yet they are genuinely praiseworthy.
description: Supererogation names actions that are morally praiseworthy but not morally required — acts that go beyond the call of duty. Throwing yourself on a grenade to save your comrades, donating a kidney to a stranger, spending years caring for a severely disabled person who is not your relative — these seem morally admirable in a way that exceeds what obligation demands. Moral theory must carve out space for this category. J.O. Urmson introduced the concept in 1958, arguing that standard moral theories — which classify actions as obligatory, permitted, or forbidden — have no room for the category of saintly or heroic acts admirable beyond what obligation requires. Utilitarian theories appear to collapse supererogation: if you can prevent more suffering by donating all surplus income to effective charities, you are simply obligated to do so, leaving no space for the "beyond duty" category. Peter Singer's famous argument draws this conclusion directly: we are obligated to give until giving more would harm us as much as it benefits others. Those who find this too demanding typically invoke agent-relative prerogatives — the moral permission to give special weight to one's own projects, relationships, and life plans — as a constraint on impartial consequentialist demands.

name: Moral Motivation
thinkerBio: David Hume (1711–1776) argued that reason alone cannot motivate and all motivation comes from passion, in A Treatise of Human Nature (1739); Immanuel Kant (1724–1804) argued that genuine moral worth requires acting from duty through reason alone — their disagreement defines the modern debate in moral psychology.
quote: Reason is, and ought only to be, the slave of the passions.

books.title: Groundwork of the Metaphysics of Morals
books.author: Immanuel Kant

description: What motivates moral action? And what should motivate it? These questions are central to moral psychology and to the evaluation of moral theories. Hume's answer: reason alone cannot motivate — it can identify means to ends but cannot itself generate ends. Moral motivation requires passion, sentiment, sympathy. We act morally because we care about others, not because we have derived a duty from logic. Kant's answer is the opposite: genuine moral worth requires acting from duty — from the recognition of the moral law as binding — rather than from inclination, sentiment, or self-interest. An action done from sympathy has no moral worth, however beneficial to others. An action done against one's inclinations, solely from recognition of duty, has full moral worth. Only rational motivation can be genuinely moral. The debate maps onto deep questions about moral psychology: are moral judgments motivationally inert beliefs (requiring separate desires to become action-guiding) or are they inherently motivating? Internalists hold that genuine moral judgment entails motivation; externalists deny this. The debate has implications for the metaethical status of moral claims and whether akrasia — knowing the right thing and not doing it — is psychologically possible.

name: The Trolley Problem's Legacy
thinkerBio: Joshua Greene (b. 1974) at Harvard developed the dual-process neuroscientific account of trolley-style moral judgment in Moral Tribes (2013); Judith Jarvis Thomson herself revisited her original cases decades later in 'The Trolley Problem Revisited.'
quote: Personal moral dilemmas engage emotional response; impersonal ones engage cold deliberation -- the trolley problem reveals two different cognitive systems at work.

books.title: Would You Kill the Fat Man?
books.author: David Edmonds

description: The trolley problem's lasting significance lies not in answering the moral questions it poses but in transforming how moral philosophy is done. Joshua Greene's neuroscientific research showed that different types of trolley cases activate different neural systems — personal cases (pushing someone) activate emotional systems; impersonal cases (pulling a lever) activate deliberative systems. This suggests that moral intuitions in trolley cases reflect dual-process cognitive architecture rather than tracking genuine moral facts. The philosophical response to Greene's "empirical debunking" of moral intuitions is contested. If our intuitive revulsion at pushing someone into a trolley's path is explained by the same neural systems that generate disgust, and if this is what our intuition is tracking rather than moral facts about using people as means, then that intuition loses its evidential force. But if intuitions are the bedrock of moral theory, debunking them requires a positive alternative. Judith Thomson's own later work — "Killing, Letting Die, and the Trolley Problem" and "The Trolley Problem Revisited" — shows how a single thought experiment can sustain forty years of productive philosophical work, generating ever-more-refined distinctions about causation, intention, rights, and the structure of moral justification. The trolley problem's legacy is methodological: it demonstrated that moral philosophy can make progress through the careful analysis of intuition-generating cases.

name: Moral Realism vs Anti-Realism
thinkerBio: Russ Shafer-Landau (b. 1963) at Wisconsin-Madison wrote the most systematic contemporary defence of moral realism; Simon Blackburn (b. 1944) represents the most sophisticated anti-realist alternative through quasi-realism.
quote: The debate between moral realism and anti-realism is the central organizing question of contemporary metaethics.

name: Substance and Accident
thinkerBio: Aristotle (384-322 BCE) developed the substance-accident distinction in the Categories and Metaphysics; it became central to medieval Scholastic philosophy and remains foundational for debates about the nature of objects and properties.
quote: Substance is that which is not predicated of anything else, but of which everything else is predicated.

name: Bundle Theory
thinkerBio: David Hume (1711-1776) developed bundle theory of the self in A Treatise of Human Nature; contemporary bundle theorists of objects more generally include Russell and, in a qualified form, Rudolf Carnap.
quote: I never can catch myself at any time without a perception, and never can observe anything but the perception.

name: The Problem of Universals
thinkerBio: Plato (428-348 BCE) defended a realist theory of Forms as universals existing independently of particulars; D.M. Armstrong (1926-2014) at Sydney developed the most influential contemporary realist alternative in A World of States of Affairs (1997).
quote: A universal is something that can be wholly present in many different places at the same time.

books.title: A World of States of Affairs
books.author: D.M. Armstrong

books.title: The Problem of Universals in Contemporary Philosophy
books.author: Gabriele Galluzzo and Michael Loux (eds.)

description: The problem of universals asks what ground the application of a general term to multiple particular things. When we say that Socrates and Plato are both wise, what makes both applications of "wise" correct? The realist answer: there is a universal, Wisdom, that both Socrates and Plato instantiate — a single entity present in (or exemplified by) both particulars. This universal is what the predicate "wise" refers to, and its presence in both explains the truth of both attributions. The nominalist denies universals: only particular things exist. Various nominalist strategies: class nominalism (both Socrates and Plato belong to the class of wise things, and that is all there is to it); resemblance nominalism (Socrates and Plato resemble each other and all other wise things — no universal is needed); trope theory (each instance of wisdom is a distinct particular — a "trope" — and the tropes of different wise things resemble each other). D.M. Armstrong defended "immanent realism": universals are real but exist only in their instances — there are no uninstantiated universals (contra Platonic realism). This view grounds the laws of nature as relations between universals and explains why laws support counterfactuals — the necessitating relation between universals holds necessarily, not contingently. Epistemology  (7 concepts in this volume)

name: Mereology
thinkerBio: Stanislaw Lesniewski (1886-1939) founded formal mereology in the 1910s; David Lewis (1941-2001) developed mereological universalism -- the view that any collection of objects composes a sum -- as part of his comprehensive metaphysical system.
quote: Composition as identity: the parts, taken together, just are the whole.
description: Mereology is the formal study of part-whole relations: what it means for one thing to be a part of another, and what conditions determine when a collection of parts constitutes a genuine whole. The discipline begins with Stanisław Leśniewski in the 1910s and has developed into a precise formal theory with deep metaphysical implications. David Lewis's mereological universalism holds that any collection of objects — however scattered, disconnected, or arbitrary — constitutes a mereological sum. The combination of this coffee cup, the Eiffel Tower, and Napoleon's left ear is a genuine object: the sum of those three things. This seems ontologically extravagant but is formally elegant — it eliminates the need for a principled account of when parts compose a whole. Every "composable" collection is already composed. The composition question — under what conditions do parts compose a whole? — is one of contemporary metaphysics' central problems. Peter van Inwagen's restricted composition holds that parts compose a whole only when they constitute a living organism; everything else we ordinarily call an object is really "simples arranged F-wise" rather than a genuine F. The debate between universalism, restricted composition, and nihilism (no composite objects exist) has implications for the ontology of ordinary objects, persons, and artifacts.

name: Grounding and Metaphysical Dependence
thinkerBio: Kit Fine (b. 1946) at NYU and Jonathan Schaffer (b. 1970) at Rutgers are the leading developers of contemporary grounding theory, which seeks to formalize the relation of metaphysical dependence underlying much philosophical explanation.
quote: Grounding is the relation that holds between a fact and the more fundamental facts that explain it.

books.title: Writing the Book of the World
books.author: Theodore Sider

name: The Mind-Body Problem
thinkerBio: Rene Descartes (1596-1650) gave the mind-body problem its canonical modern formulation through substance dualism; the problem has generated every major position in contemporary philosophy of mind from physicalism to panpsychism.
quote: How can an unextended thinking substance causally interact with an extended physical body?

name: Reliabilism
thinkerBio: Alvin Goldman (b. 1938) at Rutgers introduced process reliabilism in 'A Causal Theory of Knowing' (1967) and developed it fully in subsequent decades; he remains the most influential figure in externalist epistemology.
quote: S knows that p if S's belief that p was produced by a reliable cognitive process.

books.title: Liaisons: Philosophy Meets the Cognitive and Social Sciences
books.author: Alvin Goldman

description: Reliabilism, developed by Alvin Goldman, holds that a belief constitutes knowledge if it is true and was produced by a reliable cognitive process — a process that tends to produce true beliefs. Perception, memory, sound inference, and valid deductive reasoning are reliable processes. Wishful thinking, guessing, and reasoning from known falsehoods are not. Reliabilism replaces the internalist justification condition in traditional epistemology with an externalist reliability condition. Reliabilism is an externalist theory: whether your belief counts as knowledge depends on facts external to your perspective. You need not know that your belief was reliably produced in order for it to constitute knowledge. A child who perceives a red ball and forms the belief "there is a red ball" has knowledge, even without understanding anything about the reliability of visual perception. The theory has the advantage of explaining how animals and small children can have perceptual knowledge without the conceptual sophistication to assess their own processes. Its main challenge is the "generality problem": every token belief-forming process is an instance of many different types, with different reliability rates. Which type is relevant for assessing whether the resulting belief is justified? Without a principled answer, reliabilism cannot deliver determinate verdicts about particular cases.

name: Foundationalism
thinkerBio: Rene Descartes (1596-1650) is the paradigmatic classical foundationalist; Roderick Chisholm (1916-1999) at Brown developed the most systematic contemporary foundationalist epistemology across multiple editions of Theory of Knowledge.
quote: Knowledge must rest on a foundation of basic beliefs that are justified without depending on any other beliefs.
description: Foundationalism holds that the structure of justified belief is hierarchical: some beliefs are "basic" — justified without depending on the support of other beliefs — and all other beliefs derive their justification from these foundations, directly or indirectly. The structure resembles a building: basic beliefs are the load-bearing foundations; all other beliefs are the upper floors they support. Descartes sought incorrigible foundations — beliefs so certain they cannot be doubted, such as the cogito and the contents of clear and distinct ideas. Roderick Chisholm's modest foundationalism requires only that basic beliefs have some degree of justification in themselves — perhaps from direct perceptual experience or a priori intuition — without requiring the certainty Descartes demanded. Basic beliefs need not be infallible, just self-supporting. Foundationalism faces the dilemma of the foundation: either basic beliefs are genuinely self-justifying (but why should self-justification be epistemically acceptable?) or they are justified by something else (in which case they are not truly foundational). Coherentism rejects the hierarchical structure entirely, arguing for a web of mutually supporting beliefs with no privileged foundation. The debate remains one of epistemology's most productive fault lines.

name: Virtue Epistemology
thinkerBio: Ernest Sosa (b. 1940) at Rutgers and Linda Zagzebski (b. 1946) at Oklahoma developed virtue epistemology as a systematic alternative grounding knowledge in the exercise of intellectual virtues rather than mere reliable processes or internalist justification.
quote: Knowledge is apt belief -- true belief that manifests the cognitive virtue of the believer.
description: Virtue epistemology applies the structure of virtue ethics to the theory of knowledge: just as moral virtue ethics focuses on the traits and dispositions that constitute good character rather than on rules or outcomes, virtue epistemology focuses on the intellectual virtues — stable dispositions that make one a reliable and responsible inquirer. Ernest Sosa proposed that knowledge is "apt belief" — belief that succeeds through the manifestation of intellectual virtue. An archer's shot is "apt" if it hits the target through the exercise of skill; a belief is "apt" if it is true through the exercise of intellectual virtues such as perceptual sensitivity, mnemonic reliability, and sound inference. Linda Zagzebski developed a more comprehensive virtue epistemology: intellectual virtues include open-mindedness, intellectual courage, thoroughness, and genuine love of truth. Knowledge is a kind of cognitive contact with reality resulting from acts of intellectual virtue. Virtue epistemology responds to reliabilism's generality problem by identifying the relevant cognitive process with the exercise of a virtue — a stable, agent-relative disposition — rather than an abstractly characterized process type. It also captures the agent-relative dimension of knowledge that process reliabilism tends to flatten: knowledge involves not just true belief reliably produced but responsible, virtuous epistemic agency.

name: Testimony
thinkerBio: David Hume (1711-1776) gave the classic reductionist treatment of testimony in An Enquiry Concerning Human Understanding; C.A.J. Coady (b. 1936) defended anti-reductionism in Testimony: A Philosophical Study (1992), arguing testimony is a basic, irreducible source of knowledge.
quote: Testimony is the default-and-challenge structure of human knowledge transmission -- we believe what others tell us unless given specific reason for doubt.
description: Testimony is the primary vehicle through which knowledge is socially transmitted — most of what any of us knows, we know because someone told us. The epistemology of testimony asks: under what conditions is testimonial belief epistemically justified? And what epistemic obligations does one acquire when one gives testimony and others rely on it? Reductionists about testimony (Hume) hold that testimonial belief is justified only by positive, non-testimonial evidence for the reliability of the particular source. We cannot take testimony at face value — we must verify through inductive evidence from past experience. Anti-reductionists hold that testimonial belief is prima facie justified — innocent until proven unreliable — without needing positive evidential support. The default is trust, not suspicion. A middle position — "default and challenge" — holds that testimony generates prima facie justification that is defeated only by positive reasons for doubt. Miranda Fricker's work on testimonial injustice adds the dimension of how social identity affects the credibility granted to testifiers: a Black man reporting police misconduct, a woman reporting workplace harassment — their testimony is systematically deflated in ways that constitute a specific epistemic injustice, not merely an unfortunate bias.

name: The Problem of Induction
thinkerBio: David Hume (1711-1776) posed the problem of induction in A Treatise of Human Nature (1739); Nelson Goodman (1906-1998) deepened it dramatically with the new riddle of induction in Fact, Fiction, and Forecast (1955).
quote: Past experience can be allowed to give direct and certain information of those precise objects only, and that precise period of time which fell under its cognizance.

books.title: The Problem of Induction and Its Solution
books.author: Wesley Salmon

description: The problem of induction, posed by Hume, asks what justifies inferring a general conclusion from a finite number of observations. We observe the sun rising every day and conclude it will rise tomorrow; we observe copper conducting electricity and conclude all copper does. But past regularities provide no logical guarantee of future ones. The inference from "all observed Xs are Y" to "all Xs are Y" is not deductively valid. What is its epistemic warrant? Hume's answer: habit or custom. We are naturally disposed to project past regularities forward, but this disposition has no rational foundation. Induction is psychologically indispensable but epistemically unjustifiable by any non-circular argument. Kant's response: inductive generalization is possible because the mind imposes causal structure on experience as a precondition for coherent experience at all. Nelson Goodman showed the problem runs even deeper: even granting that induction is sometimes legitimate, how do we identify which properties are genuinely "projectible"? Everything observed before a certain time is "grue" — green if observed before that time and blue thereafter. "All emeralds are grue" is confirmed by all our observations of green emeralds just as well as "all emeralds are green." But we project only "green." Why? Goodman's "new riddle of induction" shows that induction presupposes a theory of natural kinds that cannot itself be inductively derived.

name: Social Epistemology
thinkerBio: Alvin Goldman (b. 1938) founded contemporary social epistemology with Knowledge in a Social World (1999); Helen Longino (b. 1944) developed a parallel feminist social epistemology of science emphasizing communal critical norms.
quote: Most of what any individual knows, they know because they trust others, not because they verified it themselves.
description: Social epistemology examines how social structures, institutions, and relations affect the acquisition and justification of knowledge. It asks: what epistemic norms should govern testimony, scientific communities, intellectual disagreement, and the distribution of cognitive labor? The field represents a decisive shift from epistemology's traditional focus on the isolated individual inquirer to knowledge as a fundamentally collective, socially situated enterprise. Alvin Goldman's "veritistic social epistemology" evaluates social practices and institutions by their tendency to promote true belief across a community. Helen Longino's social epistemology of science argues that objectivity is not a property of individual inquirers but of communities with appropriate critical norms: genuine venues for criticism, actual uptake of criticism by scientists, publicly recognized standards, and equal treatment of alternative hypotheses. Science's objectivity is achieved through its social structure, not despite it. Philip Kitcher showed that the division of cognitive labor — different scientists pursuing different research strategies — can benefit science as a whole even when individual scientists are motivated by reputation rather than truth-seeking. A healthy intellectual community needs productive dissent and heterodox perspectives even when the mainstream is producing excellent work. The replication crisis in psychology and social science is a prominent contemporary illustration of social epistemology's practical stakes.

name: Existential Phenomenology
thinkerBio: Maurice Merleau-Ponty (1908-1961) and Jean-Paul Sartre (1905-1980) jointly founded existential phenomenology by applying Husserl's phenomenological method to questions of embodied existence, freedom, and concrete situatedness.
quote: Existence precedes essence, but existence is always embodied, situated, and lived from the inside.
description: Existential phenomenology combines the phenomenological method — describing experience from the first-person perspective without prior theoretical commitments — with the existentialist focus on the concrete structures of human existence: freedom, finitude, facticity, and the encounter with others. It asks not "what are the formal structures of consciousness?" (Husserl) but "what is the structure of actually existing human being-in-the-world?" Sartre's Being and Nothingness (1943) is the founding text: it describes the structures of consciousness (being-for-itself) and its relation to the in-itself (things without consciousness), the facticity and transcendence of human reality, the encounter with the Other, and the fundamental project by which each person chooses themselves. The work combines rigorous phenomenological description with sweeping ontological claims. Merleau-Ponty diverged from Sartre by centering the analysis on the body rather than consciousness: the fundamental structure of human existence is not consciousness-in-a-body but an embodied subject-in-the-world. The body is not an object the mind controls but the pre-reflective vehicle of all experience and action. This shift grounds existential phenomenology in the concreteness of embodied practice rather than the abstract structures of consciousness.

name: Absurdism and Revolt
thinkerBio: Albert Camus (1913-1960) developed absurdism in The Myth of Sisyphus (1942) and extended its political dimension in The Rebel (1951); his sustained public dispute with Sartre over the latter's communism stemmed partly from this political development.
quote: There is no fate that cannot be surmounted by scorn -- one must imagine Sisyphus happy.
description: Camus's absurdism develops the implications of the absurd — the confrontation between human need for meaning and the universe's silence — into a philosophical ethics. Having rejected both religious faith (philosophical suicide) and actual suicide (physical denial of the problem), Camus proposes revolt as the authentic response: a sustained refusal to accept the terms imposed by the absurd, a passionate insistence on living in full awareness of meaninglessness. The Myth of Sisyphus (1942) develops this through the Greek myth: Sisyphus is condemned to roll his boulder up the hill forever, only to watch it roll back down. This is the image of human existence — purposeless repetition in a universe indifferent to our efforts. But Camus proposes that we must imagine Sisyphus happy: he has claimed the absurdity as his own. His revolt is not heroic triumph but lucid acceptance. The rebellious refusal to hope or resign is itself a form of dignity. The Rebel (1951) extended absurdism into political philosophy: historical revolt — the political refusal to accept oppression — must be grounded in the same lucid refusal of the absolute that characterizes metaphysical revolt. Totalitarianism fails because it replaces one absolute (God) with another (History, the Party) instead of maintaining the tension of revolt. Camus's political philosophy of "limit" — accepting finite, concrete improvements over revolutionary totalization — remains distinctive.

name: Time and Existence
thinkerBio: Henri Bergson (1859-1941) developed the philosophy of duration in Time and Free Will (1889); Martin Heidegger (1889-1976) radicalized the analysis of temporality as the fundamental structure of Dasein's existence in Being and Time (1927).
quote: Duration is the continuous progress of the past which gnaws into the future and which swells as it advances.
description: The phenomenology of time in existentialist thought is not about objective time measured by clocks but about the temporal structure of existence as lived from the inside. Henri Bergson's "duration" (durée) is the immediate flow of lived time — a continuous, undivided movement of consciousness — irreducible to the spatialized, measurable time of physics, which is always a retrospective reconstruction, not time as actually experienced. Heidegger radicalized this: the structure of Dasein is fundamentally temporal in the existential sense. Dasein is always projecting toward its future (its possibilities, including death), always returning to its past (its thrown facticity, its "having-been"), and always encountering the present as the moment of decision between its past and its future. Authentic temporality — "resoluteness" — is the integration of past, future, and present in a unified, owned existence. The implications: philosophy cannot treat time as an external container in which human beings exist. Humans exist temporally — their very being is a being-in-time that constitutes what they are. This grounds Heidegger's critique of traditional metaphysics (which treats Being as presence, ignoring time) and his claim that the "question of Being" must be approached through an analysis of the temporality of Dasein.

name: Freedom and Responsibility
thinkerBio: Jean-Paul Sartre (1905-1980) developed the existentialist account of radical freedom and total responsibility in Being and Nothingness (1943); Simone de Beauvoir (1908-1986) refined it with the concept of situated freedom in The Ethics of Ambiguity (1947).
quote: We are condemned to be free, and with this freedom comes total responsibility for what we make of ourselves.
description: For Sartre, freedom is not a property that humans have alongside other properties — it is the structure of human consciousness itself. Consciousness is not a thing but a "nothingness" that is perpetually distancing itself from what it is. This radical freedom is inseparable from radical responsibility: since we are always choosing — even in apparent passivity or inaction — we are always responsible for what we do and who we become. There is no escape into "I had no choice" or "my nature made me do it." Simone de Beauvoir complicated Sartre's account with the concept of "situation": freedom is not absolute but situated. The situation of women under patriarchy, of colonized peoples under empire, is not a neutral backdrop for free choice but a structure that actively limits freedom — not by reducing it to zero but by systematically closing off possibilities and shaping the very framework within which choices are made. Freedom without appropriate material and social conditions is abstract and hollow. The ethical dimension: if we are radically free, what does this require of us morally? Sartre's existentialist ethics grounds morality in the recognition of others' freedom: in choosing for myself, I implicitly choose for all humans, endorsing a model of human existence. To oppress another is to deny their freedom while relying on freedom in one's own self-constitution — a contradiction that is both logical and ethical.

name: Death and Dying
thinkerBio: Epicurus (341-270 BCE) developed the symmetry argument that death cannot harm the one who dies; Martin Heidegger (1889-1976) and Bernard Williams (1929-2003) developed the most influential modern challenges to Epicurean consolation.
quote: Death, the most terrifying of ills, is nothing to us, since so long as we exist, death is not with us; but when death comes, then we do not exist.
description: The philosophy of death asks what death is, whether it can harm the person who dies, and how the prospect of death should shape how we live. Epicurus's "symmetry argument" is the classic attempt to remove the sting: death cannot harm the person who dies because harm requires a subject to be harmed, and death eliminates the subject. The state of being dead is no worse than the state of not yet having been born, which we do not regard as bad. Heidegger argued against such consolation: being-toward-death is not the event of dying but the structure of existing as a being for whom death is always already one's ownmost, most certain, and non-transferable possibility. To "run ahead" in thought to death — to genuinely own it as one's own — is not morbid but clarifying. It individualizes Dasein, revealing the groundlessness of the "they-self" and creating the possibility of authentic existence. Thomas Nagel and Bernard Williams pressed the intuitive view that death is indeed bad for the person who dies: Nagel through the "deprivation account" (death deprives us of goods we would otherwise have had) and Williams through the concept of "categorical desires" (death is bad because it frustrates desires that are not conditional on continued life but that give life its structure). The debate between Epicurus and his modern opponents remains one of philosophy's most practically urgent.

name: Inauthenticity and the They-Self
thinkerBio: Martin Heidegger (1889-1976) introduced das Man (the they-self) and inauthenticity in Being and Time (1927) as the default, anonymous mode of everyday existence from which authentic existence must be actively won.
quote: Everyone is the other, and no one is himself -- the they-self, which everyday Dasein is, is the nobody.
description: The "they-self" (das Man) is Heidegger's term for the anonymous, dispersed mode of existence in which Dasein loses itself in the public world. "One does not do that," "one says," "one thinks" — the subject of these propositions is nobody in particular and everybody in general: the social average, the conventional, the undifferentiated "they." In this mode, Dasein does not take up its existence as genuinely its own but simply goes along with what the crowd does, thinks, and values. Heidegger is careful not to dismiss the they-self as simply bad: it is an essential existential structure of Dasein, not a corruption imposed from outside. We necessarily inherit language, practices, and self-understandings from a social world we did not create. The they-self is not a moral failing but an ontological possibility — and one that tends to become the default because it relieves Dasein of the burden of owning its own existence. Inauthenticity, strictly speaking, is not moral badness but ontological lostness — a failure to take up one's own existence as one's own. The transition to authenticity does not involve rejecting the they-self entirely (impossible, since we are social beings) but appropriating one's situation from the standpoint of genuine ownership — no longer living at the crowd's pace but from one's own finite, thrown, mortal perspective. Logic & Language  (7 concepts in this volume)

name: Frege's Sense and Reference
thinkerBio: Gottlob Frege (1848-1925) introduced the sense-reference distinction in 'On Sense and Reference' (1892), one of the most influential papers in the history of philosophy of language and the founding text of analytic philosophy of language.
quote: The reference of a sign is the object it designates; the sense is the manner in which the object is presented.

name: Verificationism
thinkerBio: The logical positivists of the Vienna Circle -- including Moritz Schlick (1882-1936) and Rudolf Carnap (1891-1970) -- developed verificationism as the criterion of cognitive meaningfulness; A.J. Ayer (1910-1989) popularized it in English in Language, Truth and Logic (1936).
quote: A statement is meaningful if and only if it is either analytically true or empirically verifiable.

books.title: The Logical Structure of the World
books.author: Rudolf Carnap

name: Conventionalism
thinkerBio: Henri Poincare (1854-1912) developed geometric conventionalism in Science and Hypothesis (1902); Rudolf Carnap (1891-1970) extended conventionalism to the choice of logical and linguistic frameworks more generally.
quote: One geometry cannot be more true than another; it can only be more convenient.

name: Anarchism
thinkerBio: Pierre-Joseph Proudhon (1809-1865) coined the term in What Is Property? (1840); Mikhail Bakunin (1814-1876) and Peter Kropotkin (1842-1921) developed collectivist and communist anarchism respectively, grounding political order in voluntary cooperation rather than state coercion.
quote: Property is theft -- and the state, which protects property, is the instrument of that theft.

books.title: Mutual Aid: A Factor of Evolution
books.author: Peter Kropotkin
