description: Propositional attitudes are mental states characterized by their content — a proposition that the subject stands in some attitude toward. Believing that it will rain, hoping that the exam goes well, fearing that the bridge will collapse, desiring that one's child be happy — all are propositional attitudes: attitudes (belief, hope, fear, desire) directed at propositions (that it will rain, etc.). Frege noted the opacity of propositional attitude reports: even if "Hesperus" and "Phosphorus" refer to the same planet, "John believes Hesperus is visible" and "John believes Phosphorus is visible" can differ in truth value — John may not know they are the same. This shows that in propositional attitude contexts, words are not simply about their reference but about their sense or cognitive significance. The problem of propositional attitude reports connects to debates about the relationship between language and thought, the nature of mental content, and whether concepts are individuated by their reference (externalism) or by their cognitive role (internalism). Naturalizing propositional attitudes — explaining what beliefs and desires are in purely physical terms — is one of the central challenges for physicalist philosophy of mind. Without an account of propositional attitudes, there is no adequate account of intentionality.

name: Meaning as Use
description: "The meaning of a word is its use in the language" — Wittgenstein's slogan from the Philosophical Investigations captures his rejection of the view that meaning consists in mental images, private sensations, or abstract reference to objects. Meaning is not something hidden behind language, accessible only to the speaker in their private mental life — it is public, observable, and constituted by how expressions are actually used within a practice. This has several consequences. There can be no genuinely private language: if meaning is use, and use is a public practice that requires the possibility of correction, then a language whose expressions only one person could understand in principle is not a language at all. It lacks the normative structure — the distinction between correct and incorrect use — that makes something a language rather than mere behavior. The private language argument is one of Wittgenstein's most influential and contested contributions. The view connects to the dissolution of philosophical problems: if meaning is use, then philosophical confusions arise from words being used outside the practices that give them meaning — lifted out of their language game and asked to do impossible work. The therapy for philosophical confusion is not to find better theories but to return words to their ordinary homes, where philosophical paradox dissolves.

name: The Causal Theory of Reference
description: The causal theory of reference holds that the reference of names and natural kind terms is determined not by descriptive meaning in the speaker's head but by a causal-historical chain connecting the use of the term to an initial "baptism" — an original act of naming or pointing that fixes what the term refers to. When I use the name "Napoleon," my use refers to Napoleon not because I have a complete description of him in mind but because my use is causally connected, through a chain of communicative uses, to the historical person who was originally introduced as "Napoleon." Hilary Putnam extended this to natural kind terms through his "Twin Earth" thought experiment. Imagine a planet exactly like Earth except that the substance in rivers and lakes that they call "water" has a different chemical composition — XYZ, not H2O. Putnam argues that "water" on Earth and "water" on Twin Earth refer to different substances, even though speakers on both planets have identical mental representations. "Meanings ain't in the head" — meaning is partly constituted by what is actually out there in the world. The causal theory explains how we can refer to things we cannot fully describe, how scientific discoveries can change what we know about the nature of what our terms refer to without changing which things they refer to, and why two people who know different things about gold are both talking about gold.

name: Contextualism
description: Epistemic contextualism holds that the truth conditions of knowledge attributions — sentences of the form "S knows that P" — vary with the context of the attributor rather than the epistemic situation of the subject. In ordinary, low-stakes contexts, the standards for "knowing" are low. In philosophical or high-stakes contexts, the standards rise. The same sentence "I know I have hands" can be truly uttered in ordinary conversation and falsely uttered in the context of considering Cartesian skepticism — not because my epistemic situation changes but because what counts as knowledge changes with the context of attribution. Keith DeRose developed the view partly to dissolve skeptical paradoxes without either accepting skepticism or dismissing it as confused. The skeptic is not wrong to say, in their context, that we don't "know" things — those standards are genuinely in play. But we are not wrong to claim knowledge in ordinary contexts either. The paradox dissolves once we recognize that "know" is context-sensitive. Contextualism is controversial partly because it seems to evade rather than answer skepticism — if the skeptic and the ordinary speaker are simply talking about different things, no genuine disagreement is resolved. And it raises the question of whether epistemic standards are genuinely conventional or whether there is a context-independent fact about what really counts as knowledge.

name: Formal Logic
description: Formal logic is the discipline that studies valid patterns of inference using symbolic notation to abstract away from content and represent structure alone. The validity of an argument is a matter of its logical form: "All men are mortal; Socrates is a man; therefore Socrates is mortal" is valid because any argument of the form "All As are B; C is A; therefore C is B" preserves truth regardless of what A, B, and C denote. Gottlob Frege's Begriffsschrift (1879) invented modern symbolic logic — the quantifier-variable notation that replaced Aristotle's syllogistic and made it possible to represent the logical structure of mathematical reasoning. Bertrand Russell and Alfred North Whitehead's Principia Mathematica attempted to reduce all of mathematics to logic, using Frege's notation and Russell's theory of types to avoid paradoxes. Gödel's incompleteness theorems (1931) showed that any sufficiently powerful formal system that is consistent must contain true statements that cannot be proved within that system. This result — that formal completeness and consistency cannot be simultaneously achieved in any system capable of expressing arithmetic — is one of the most profound discoveries in the history of human thought, with implications for the nature of mathematical truth, the limits of computation, and the relationship between proof and truth.

name: Analytic / Synthetic Distinction
description: The analytic-synthetic distinction, introduced by Kant, divides all propositions into two categories. Analytic propositions are true by virtue of meaning alone — the predicate is contained in the concept of the subject: "All bachelors are unmarried," "All bodies are extended." They are true by definition, adding no new information. Synthetic propositions go beyond what is contained in the subject concept and require empirical verification: "All bodies have weight," "Water boils at 100°C." Kant used this distinction to frame his central epistemological question: how is synthetic a priori knowledge possible — knowledge that is both informationally substantive and independent of experience? His answer (space, time, and the categories of the understanding are synthetic a priori) grounded his transcendental idealism. W.V.O. Quine's "Two Dogmas of Empiricism" (1951) attacked the distinction as philosophically untenable: there is no sharp boundary between truths that hold come what may (analytic) and truths that are revisable in light of experience (synthetic). Any statement can be revised in principle if we are willing to change enough of our other commitments. The web of belief faces experience as a whole. Quine's attack was enormously influential, dissolving the distinction that had structured much of modern philosophy.

name: Possible Language Games
description: One of Wittgenstein's most important applications of the language game concept is his analysis of what it would mean for something to count as following a rule. When I add 2 to a series of numbers, what makes it the case that I am following the rule "add 2" rather than some other rule that coincidentally yields the same outputs for all past cases but diverges at some large number? No finite set of past applications determines the rule; the rule extends to infinitely many new cases. Saul Kripke's interpretation of Wittgenstein as advancing a "skeptical paradox" about rule-following became enormously influential: there is no fact of the matter about what a rule requires in new cases — neither in one's past behavior nor in any "mental state." The "skeptical solution" is that attributing rule-following is a social practice: the community accepts a person as following a rule when their responses agree with the community's responses in enough cases. The implications for philosophy of mind and philosophy of mathematics are profound: if there are no mental facts that determine the meaning of one's words and the rules one is following, then meaning is constitutively social and public rather than private and individual. This connects to debates about whether meanings are in the head, whether thought requires language, and what grounds the normativity of rational inference. Political Philosophy  (8 concepts in this volume)

name: Self-Consciousness and Apperception
description: Self-consciousness — the mind's capacity to be aware of itself as an experiencing subject — is both philosophically fundamental and deeply puzzling. For Kant, the "I think" that must be able to accompany all my representations is the transcendental unity of apperception: a formal condition for the unity of experience, not an empirical self discoverable through introspection. It is not a special experience but the logical presupposition of all experience having a unified subject. Fichte radicalized this: the primordial act of the self is the "I's" self-positing — a spontaneous self-constituting act that cannot be explained by anything prior. The self does not discover itself; it creates itself through the original act of self-positing. This makes self-consciousness not a property of a pre-existing subject but the very act that first constitutes subjectivity, and it makes the self's relation to itself — its self-knowledge — the foundation of all knowledge. Contemporary discussions distinguish phenomenal self-consciousness (the felt quality of being oneself), access self-consciousness (the availability of one's mental states to introspective report), and narrative self-consciousness (the extended sense of oneself as a continuing agent through time). These may be neurologically dissociable: certain conditions disrupt some while preserving others, suggesting distinct underlying mechanisms rather than a single faculty of self-awareness.

name: The Binding Problem
description: The binding problem asks how the brain combines separately processed features of a scene — the color, shape, motion, and location of an object processed in different cortical areas — into a unified, coherent perceptual experience. When I see a red ball moving to my left, the redness, the roundness, the motion, and the location are processed in anatomically separate brain regions. How are these separate processing streams "bound" together into the experience of a single object with all these properties? Anne Treisman's "feature integration theory" proposed that attention is the mechanism of binding: features are processed in parallel, pre-attentively, but are bound into objects by the spotlight of attention scanning the scene. This explains phenomena like "illusory conjunctions" — perceiving the features of different objects incorrectly combined when attention is overloaded. The binding problem connects to the hard problem: even if we solve the neural binding problem — identifying the mechanism that creates unified neural representations — this does not explain why those unified representations are accompanied by unified subjective experience. The binding problem is the neural correlate of phenomenal unity — the fact that my experience of the red ball is a single, integrated experience rather than separate experiences of redness, roundness, and motion. Eastern Philosophy  (7 concepts in this volume)

name: Aesthetic Judgment
description: Aesthetic judgment — the judgment that something is beautiful, sublime, graceful, elegant, or moving — has a peculiar status: it is felt as objective ("this painting is beautiful") yet cannot be verified empirically or proved by argument. This combination of subjective feeling and claimed intersubjective validity is the defining puzzle of aesthetics. Hume acknowledged "the standard of taste" as a problem: although taste is ultimately founded in sentiment, not all sentiments are equally reliable. The ideal critic — who has wide experience, refined sensibility, freedom from prejudice, and the capacity for comparison — represents the standard to which taste aspires. Aesthetic disagreement often reflects differences in experience, attention, or freedom from bias rather than genuine incommensurability. Kant's analysis in the Critique of Judgment remains the most rigorous: a judgment of beauty claims universal validity — "this is beautiful" means more than "I like this" — yet cannot be grounded in concepts or proved by argument. It arises from the "free harmonious play" of imagination and understanding, producing a pleasure that we demand others share. The universality is "subjective" (grounded in the shared cognitive structure of all human judges) rather than "objective" (grounded in properties of the object), but this does not reduce it to mere personal preference.

name: The Philosophy of Music
description: What does music mean, and can it represent or express anything beyond the organization of pure sound? Eduard Hanslick's formalism held that music is "tonally moving forms" — pure structure and pattern of sound with no semantic content beyond itself. Attempts to hear music as "about" emotions, storms, or pastoral scenes are confused projections of listeners rather than properties of the music itself. Music has no subject matter. The expressiveness of music is harder to deny: music unmistakably sounds sad, joyful, melancholic, or triumphant. Peter Kivy's "contour theory" accounts for this: music is expressive not because it expresses the composer's feelings or induces them in listeners but because it resembles — in contour, rhythm, and pace — the characteristic behavioral and vocal patterns of emotional expression. Music sounds sad because it has the movement profile of a sad person. Jerrold Levinson argues for "hypothetical intentionalism" in music: understanding music involves imagining a persona expressing the music — treating the sounds as if they were an utterance by a human agent. This explains why musical understanding requires cultural and historical context: to hear Bach is to hear music as an eighteenth-century contrapuntist might have expressed himself, and this context is constitutive of the musical meaning. Philosophy of Science  (7 concepts in this volume)

name: Reductionism
description: Reductionism holds that the theories and entities of the special sciences — biology, psychology, chemistry, economics — can in principle be reduced to more fundamental physical theories and entities. Ernest Nagel's formal model of reduction requires that the laws of the higher-level theory be derivable, with appropriate bridge principles connecting the vocabularies, from the laws of the lower-level theory. Chemistry reduces to physics; biology reduces to chemistry and physics; psychology reduces to neuroscience. Multiple realizability (Putnam) is the strongest objection: higher-level properties can be realized by different physical configurations in different organisms, undermining the idea that a given higher-level type maps onto a single physical type. If "pain" covers an open-ended range of physical implementations, there is no strict reduction available. Jerry Fodor's "special sciences" argument extends this: the laws of psychology, economics, and biology carve nature at different joints than physics does, making their theories autonomous and irreducible. Antireductionists argue for "emergent" properties — consciousness is the paradigm case — that are novel and irreducible to their physical substrates. The debate about reductionism is central to the unity of science and to whether we can ultimately have a single, unified account of all phenomena.

name: Emergence
description: Emergence refers to the appearance of properties, patterns, or behaviors at higher levels of organization that are not present in, directly predictable from, or reducible to the properties of lower-level components in isolation. Water is wet; neither hydrogen nor oxygen alone is wet. Consciousness is present in a functioning brain; no individual neuron is conscious. These properties seem to emerge from organization rather than being straightforwardly derivable from components. Weak emergence: higher-level properties can in principle be derived from lower-level ones given complete information and unlimited computational resources, but the derivation is practically intractable — too complex to perform. Strong emergence: higher-level properties are not even in principle derivable from lower-level ones; they are genuinely novel features of the world that come into existence at higher organizational levels. Weak emergence is scientifically mainstream: the wetness of water can in principle be derived from quantum mechanics, even if the derivation is enormously complex. Strong emergence is philosophically radical: it would mean that the physical level does not fully determine all higher-level facts, contradicting physicalist metaphysics. David Chalmers argues consciousness is a case of strong emergence — which is why the hard problem resists all physicalist solutions that explain neural function without explaining phenomenal experience.

name: Laws of Nature
description: What distinguishes a law of nature from a mere coincidence? Both "all copper conducts electricity" and "all the coins currently in my pocket are silver" may be true universal generalizations — but only the first is a law of nature. Laws support counterfactual conditionals ("if this were copper, it would conduct electricity"), serve in scientific explanations, and hold with a kind of necessity that accidents lack. The regularity view (Hume's descendants, including Lewis's Best System Analysis): laws are simply true universal generalizations — or more precisely, the generalizations that appear in the optimal systematization of all natural facts, balancing maximal strength with maximal simplicity. Necessity is not "in the world" but reflects the privileged place of certain generalizations in our best theoretical system. The necessitarian view (Armstrong, Dretske, Tooley): laws involve genuine necessity — they are second-order relations between universals (properties), not merely facts about particular instances. "All copper conducts electricity" expresses a necessitating relation between the universal "being copper" and the universal "conducting electricity." This explains why laws support counterfactuals and accidents don't, but requires positing universals as metaphysical entities and answering the question of how these second-order facts are known.

name: Scientific Method
description: The scientific method refers to the systematic procedures through which scientists generate, test, and validate knowledge claims about the natural world. Francis Bacon's inductivism: science proceeds by gathering large numbers of observations, identifying patterns and regularities, and generalizing to theories. The scientist should approach nature with an "open mind," free from preconceptions, following the evidence wherever it leads. Karl Popper's hypothetico-deductivism replaced Baconian inductivism: scientists form bold hypotheses — conjectural answers to scientific questions — and deduce observable predictions from them. Confirming predictions provides evidence for the hypothesis; disconfirming predictions refutes it. Science proceeds by conjecture and refutation, not by accumulation of confirming instances. Imre Lakatos's methodology of scientific research programs introduced a more sophisticated picture: scientific rationality operates at the level of research programs over time, not individual hypothesis tests. A program is progressive if it generates novel successful predictions; it is degenerative if its modifications are merely defensive. Paul Feyerabend's provocative "anything goes" position argued that all methodological rules have been violated during actual scientific revolutions, suggesting that no single methodology constrains scientific progress.

name: Causation in Science
description: The concept of causation is indispensable in science — experiments aim to establish causal rather than merely correlational relationships, and scientific explanations typically proceed by identifying causes. Yet the metaphysics of causation remains contested, and the question of how causal claims are established and used in science raises distinct methodological questions. James Woodward's interventionist account of causation defines causes in terms of interventions: X causes Y if and only if an ideal intervention on X (one that changes X without affecting Y through any path other than through X) would change Y. This account is well-suited to experimental science, where causal claims are established precisely through controlled interventions. It also connects causation to a notion of manipulation: causes are variables that, if we could intervene on them, would produce changes in their effects. Nancy Cartwright's "capacities" account holds that causes work by triggering stable capacities of things — not through exceptionless universal laws but through the exercise of dispositions that may be masked by countervailing factors in particular circumstances. This captures how causal generalizations in the special sciences (social science, medicine, economics) can be true even when they admit of exceptions, and why explanation often requires understanding mechanisms rather than merely subsuming events under laws.

name: Demarcation Problem
description: The demarcation problem asks: what distinguishes genuine science from pseudoscience, metaphysics, and non-science? The question matters practically — it determines what should be taught in science classes, what courts should accept as expert testimony, and what research deserves public funding. It matters philosophically — it forces clarification of what makes scientific inquiry epistemically distinctive. Popper's answer was falsifiability: a theory is scientific only if it makes predictions that could in principle be proven false. This was designed to exclude psychoanalysis and Marxist historical theory while including physics. But falsifiability proved too simple: many accepted scientific theories are not straightforwardly falsifiable (string theory, certain cosmological models), and some pseudosciences can be reformulated to make falsifiable predictions. Lakatos proposed that demarcation operates at the level of research programs over time: a progressive program that generates novel successful predictions is scientific; a degenerative program that only makes ad hoc adjustments to accommodate counterexamples is pseudoscientific. Most philosophers now accept that no sharp, principled demarcation between science and non-science is achievable, that the question admits of degrees, and that the criteria (empirical accountability, connection to well-confirmed theory, methodological rigor) apply in family-resemblance rather than all-or-nothing fashion. Continental Philosophy  (7 concepts in this volume)

name: Power-Knowledge
description: Foucault's concept of power-knowledge (pouvoir-savoir) holds that knowledge and power are not external to each other — power relations produce knowledge, and knowledge always involves the exercise of power. There is no neutral truth accessible from outside power relations; truth is produced within them. Every regime of truth — every set of norms determining what counts as true and what methods count as legitimate — is simultaneously a regime of power. This does not mean all knowledge is false or merely political. It means that the categories, methods, and questions constituting any discipline reflect the power relations within which the discipline developed and operates. Psychiatry's power to determine who is "sane" and who is "mad" is not separate from its medical knowledge — the knowledge and the power are the same practice. The prison's power to punish is inseparable from criminological knowledge about the criminal type. Foucault's genealogical method traced the historical emergence of power-knowledge regimes: how madness came to be defined as a medical condition requiring professional treatment, how sexuality came to be organized around the category of "perversion" in the nineteenth century, how criminality came to require a "soul" to punish rather than merely a body to discipline. Genealogy's purpose is to reveal contingency — things could have been otherwise — and thereby open spaces for resistance.

name: Moral Particularism
description: Moral particularism, developed by Jonathan Dancy, holds that the moral relevance of any feature of a situation is not constant across cases but varies with context. A feature that counts in favor of an action in one situation may count against it in another, or be entirely morally neutral in a third. There are no genuine exceptionless moral principles of the form "whenever F is present, there is a reason to do A," because whether F is a reason — and in which direction it points — depends entirely on what other features are present alongside it. This is a direct challenge to the entire project of moral theory as traditionally conceived: the project of identifying principles that articulate the constant moral relevance of features across cases. Dancy argues that moral knowledge is not knowledge of principles but a developed perceptual capacity — a refined ability to see how all the features of a particular situation hang together and what they jointly require. Good moral reasoning is holistic, not algorithmic. Moral generalism — the mainstream view that principles exist and are necessary for moral thought — responds that particularism cannot account for moral learning, moral argument, or moral teaching, all of which seem to require generalizations. The debate turns on whether principles are presupposed by competent moral reasoning or whether they are merely helpful heuristics that can always be overridden by particularist perception of the situation.

name: Autonomy and Paternalism
description: Paternalism is the practice of limiting a person's freedom for their own good — overriding their autonomous choices on the grounds that those choices are harmful to themselves, or that the person lacks the information or capacity to choose well. The paradigm cases are legal prohibitions on self-harmful behavior: motorcycle helmet laws, drug prohibitions, mandatory seatbelt laws. The philosophical question: when, if ever, is this justified? Mill's harm principle provides the classical liberal answer: the only legitimate ground for restricting individual liberty is to prevent harm to others. Preventing self-harm cannot justify state interference; each person is the best judge of their own interests. This "anti-paternalist" position is grounded in both epistemic humility (we often cannot know what is good for someone better than they do) and respect for autonomy as intrinsically valuable. Joel Feinberg introduced the distinction between "hard" paternalism (overriding genuinely autonomous choices) and "soft" paternalism (intervening only when choices are substantially non-autonomous — made under ignorance, coercion, or impaired judgment). Soft paternalism is broadly accepted; hard paternalism is contested. The debate sharpens around cases of addiction, mental illness, and self-destructive choices made from adaptive preferences shaped by oppression.

name: Complicity and Moral Responsibility
description: The ethics of complicity asks when an agent bears moral responsibility for harms caused primarily by another. We intuitively hold that a person who knowingly assists, enables, or benefits from a wrongdoing is morally implicated in it — not merely as an unfortunate bystander but as a participant who shares responsibility. The philosophical challenge is articulating exactly what makes someone complicit and what degree of moral weight this responsibility carries. Christopher Kutz's "complicity principle" holds that an agent is accountable for a collectively harmful outcome when they act with the intention of contributing to it — even if their individual contribution is causally insignificant. A single member of a bombing squad who drops a bomb that causes no damage is still complicit in the collective harm produced by the squad as a whole. Participation in a harmful collective enterprise generates accountability regardless of individual causal efficacy. Larry May's work on collective responsibility extended this to group membership: belonging to a group that commits harm, and benefiting from that group membership, generates a form of moral taint that creates obligations of reparation and remediation even for members who did not personally participate. This grounds obligations of wealthy nations to address colonial harms and obligations of majority groups to address systemic discrimination.

name: Moral Progress
description: Moral progress asks whether the expansion of moral concern, the reduction of cruelty, and the growth of human rights represent genuine improvement — not just change — and what philosophical account makes sense of this. The claim that abolishing slavery, extending suffrage to women, and prohibiting torture represent improvements rather than merely changes requires some account of what makes a moral change progressive rather than merely different. Moral realists can account for moral progress directly: we are getting closer to moral truth, just as science gets closer to empirical truth. Peter Singer's "expanding circle" narrative describes the progressive extension of moral concern from family, to tribe, to nation, to species, to all sentient beings as genuine progress enabled by reason's capacity to recognize the arbitrary basis of previous exclusions. Richard Rorty's neo-pragmatist account bypasses moral realism: progress is expansion of sympathy — the extension of "us" to include previously excluded groups. This does not require discovering universal moral truths but only recognizing that suffering matters regardless of the group membership of the sufferer. Steven Pinker's empirical argument adds that long-term quantitative trends — declining rates of violence, expanding rights, reducing cruelty — constitute measurable evidence for moral progress, whatever its ultimate philosophical grounding.

name: Moral Uncertainty
description: Moral uncertainty — not knowing which moral theory or principle is correct — is the normal condition of careful moral reasoners. We face genuine uncertainty about whether consequences are all that matter, whether there are absolute deontological constraints, how to weigh animal suffering against human welfare, and what we owe to future generations. How should agents act under this uncertainty? William MacAskill and Toby Ord have developed systematic approaches to "acting under moral uncertainty." One approach: "maximize expected choice-worthiness" — assign probabilities to moral theories and take the weighted average of what each recommends. This treats moral theories like empirical hypotheses that can be assigned credences. Another approach: "moral hedging" — avoid actions condemned as seriously wrong by multiple moral theories, even if permitted by one's best current theory. The philosophical difficulty: unlike empirical uncertainty, moral theories disagree about the very currency by which they should be compared. Utilitarian and Kantian theories do not simply recommend different actions but operate with entirely different frameworks for moral evaluation. Aggregating across them requires a meta-ethical framework for comparing incommensurable moral views — a framework whose own credentials are themselves uncertain.

name: Moral Psychology
description: Moral psychology examines the psychological mechanisms underlying moral judgment, motivation, and behavior. Jonathan Haidt's social intuitionist model challenged the rationalist assumption that moral judgments are the products of reasoning: his experimental work showed that people make rapid moral judgments intuitively and then construct post-hoc rationalizations. Moral reasoning is primarily a vehicle for social persuasion and self-justification rather than for reaching true moral conclusions. This connects directly to Hume's view that reason is slave to the passions — the emotional response comes first, reason serves it. Against this, Kant insisted that genuine moral action must be based on rational reflection rather than impulse or sentiment. The empirical findings of contemporary moral psychology seem to support the Humean side, but this faces the normative objection: even if we typically reason post-hoc, we ought to reason better. Aristotle's moral psychology is more sophisticated than either extreme: moral virtue requires not just right action but the right emotional response — the practically wise person feels the right things at the right times. Virtue is not the triumph of reason over emotion but the integration of both in a well-formed character. Contemporary dual-process theories of moral judgment attempt to honor both the role of fast, intuitive moral responses and the role of deliberate reasoning in moral life.

name: Forgiveness
description: Forgiveness is a complex moral practice involving the release of resentment toward someone who has wronged you. It is distinct from excusing (the wrongdoer wasn't really responsible), condoning (the wrong wasn't really wrong), and forgetting (simply ceasing to remember). Genuine forgiveness involves acknowledging the wrong and the wrongdoer's responsibility — acknowledging the legitimacy of resentment — and then choosing to release it for morally appropriate reasons. Jeffrie Murphy's influential analysis grounds resentment as a morally appropriate self-protective response to being wronged: it expresses self-respect and recognition of one's own dignity as a person with rights. Forgiveness without repentance from the wrongdoer risks self-abasement — treating the violation of one's dignity as if it did not matter. Forgiveness is most legitimate when the wrongdoer has genuinely repented, made reparation, and shown commitment to change. Charles Griswold's account connects forgiveness to a joint narrative process: forgiveness involves both parties working to reframe the relationship and the wrongdoing in a way that enables renewed relationship. Unconditional or therapeutic forgiveness — forgiving for one's own psychological benefit regardless of the wrongdoer's response — is philosophically distinct from this transactional account. The tension between these conceptions has direct implications for truth and reconciliation processes after political atrocity.

name: Consequentialism and Its Limits
description: Bernard Williams's critique of consequentialism in Utilitarianism: For and Against (1973) identified two fundamental objections. The first concerns integrity: consequentialism requires agents to calculate and maximize good outcomes regardless of the agent's own commitments, projects, and relationships. This alienates agents from their own lives — when Jim is told to shoot one innocent person to prevent a soldier from shooting twenty, consequentialism says "obviously, shoot" — but this ignores that Jim's act is Jim's act, not merely a contribution to a causal outcome. The second objection concerns negative responsibility: consequentialism holds us responsible for what we allow to happen through inaction as much as for what we cause through action. Williams argued this conflates "doing" and "allowing" in a morally illegitimate way, eliminating the intuitive distinction between what we are obligated to prevent and what we are forbidden to cause. Amartya Sen's work on capabilities and development shows that strict consequentialism fails to capture the plurality of things that matter for human well-being. A single-minded focus on maximizing welfare misses the intrinsic value of freedom, agency, rights, and procedural fairness — things that matter independently of their contribution to aggregate welfare. Sen's capabilities approach represents a consequentialist-adjacent but non-reductive account of what development and justice require. Metaphysics  (7 concepts in this volume)

name: Emergence and Supervenience
description: Supervenience is a relation of covariation: the mental supervenes on the physical if there can be no mental difference without a physical difference. More generally, A-properties supervene on B-properties if any two things that are indiscernible with respect to B-properties are also indiscernible with respect to A-properties. Supervenience captures the idea of dependence — the mental depends on the physical — without committing to reduction. Jaegwon Kim argued that supervenience without reduction is unstable: if mental events are not reducible to physical events, they cannot play any causal role in the physical world — because the physical world is causally closed, any physical effect has a sufficient physical cause. Mental events either causally overdetermine their effects (implausibly) or are causally epiphenomenal — they happen alongside physical causes but don't do any causal work. This is the "exclusion problem." Emergence offers a potential response: genuinely emergent properties have causal powers that their physical bases lack, enabling them to have distinctive causal effects without reducing to the physical. But "strong emergence" — the kind that could solve Kim's exclusion problem — requires genuine novelty at higher levels, which sits uneasily with physical causal closure. The tension between supervenience-based physicalism and the causal efficacy of the mental remains the deepest problem in philosophy of mind.

name: Free Will and Moral Responsibility
description: The metaphysics of free will is inseparable from the ethics of moral responsibility: if determinism is true and agents could not have done otherwise, can punishment, praise, and blame be justified? Peter Strawson's "Freedom and Resentment" (1962) reframed the debate: our practices of holding each other responsible — expressing resentment, indignation, gratitude, and love — are not grounded in metaphysical facts about causation but in our participation in the "reactive attitudes" that constitute human relationships. Harry Frankfurt challenged the "Principle of Alternative Possibilities" — the assumption that moral responsibility requires the ability to do otherwise. His cases: imagine a neuroscientist who would intervene to ensure you make a particular decision if you showed signs of deciding otherwise, but who never needs to intervene because you decide that way freely. You could not have done otherwise (the neuroscientist would have ensured it), yet you seem fully responsible for your decision. Peter van Inwagen's "consequence argument" defends incompatibilism: if determinism is true, our actions are the consequences of the laws of nature and events in the remote past. These are not up to us. Therefore our actions are not up to us. Compatibilists respond that "up to us" need not mean "could have been otherwise in the same physical circumstances" but something like "flows from our own character, values, and deliberation."

name: Modality and Necessity
description: Modal metaphysics studies the properties of necessity and possibility — what must be the case, what could be the case, and what cannot be the case. Every metaphysical position implicitly commits to modal claims: physicalism holds that mental facts are necessarily determined by physical facts; essentialism holds that some properties are essential (necessarily had) by their bearers; moral realism holds that at least some moral facts hold necessarily. Kripke's distinction between metaphysical necessity (what is the case in all possible worlds, given the actual nature of things) and conceptual necessity (what is guaranteed by the meanings of terms) opened a new space for metaphysics: there can be necessary truths that are not analytic and not a priori. "Gold is the element with atomic number 79" is necessarily true (given what gold actually is) but was discovered empirically. Kit Fine argued that Kripke's analysis of essence in terms of necessity is inadequate: "Socrates is necessarily such that he is a member of the set {Socrates}" is necessarily true but is not part of Socrates's essence — it concerns the set, not Socrates. Fine proposed that essence should be understood as "what it is to be" something — a richer, more specific notion than mere necessary truth. This refinement has reshaped the contemporary metaphysics of essence, kinds, and objects.

name: The Nature of Truth
description: What is truth? Correspondence theories hold that a proposition is true if and only if it corresponds to a fact in reality — there is something in the world that makes it true. This intuitive view faces the "truthmaker" problem: what exactly are facts, and how does correspondence work? Coherence theories hold that truth consists in coherence with a system of other beliefs — a view attractive to idealists but vulnerable to the isolation objection (a coherent fiction is not thereby true). Pragmatist theories (James, Dewey): truth is what works — what proves useful to believe. A belief is true if holding it enables successful action and prediction. Deflationism (Ramsey, Quine, Horwich): the truth predicate adds no substantive property to what is said. "Snow is white is true" says nothing more than "snow is white." The truth predicate is a device for generalization and endorsement, not a real property. Deflationism is enormously influential in contemporary analytic philosophy. Alfred Tarski's formal definition — the T-schema: "P" is true if and only if P — captures the logical structure of truth without committing to any substantive metaphysical account. Truth is disquotational: to say a sentence is true is to say what the sentence says. Contemporary debates ask whether deflationism can account for all the explanatory roles truth plays in semantics, science, and action theory.

name: Phenomenal Consciousness and the Physical
description: The relationship between phenomenal consciousness — the qualitative, "what it's like" dimension of experience — and the physical world generates some of the deepest problems in contemporary metaphysics. Joseph Levine introduced the concept of the "explanatory gap": even if we accept that pain is identical to C-fiber firing, we cannot explain why C-fiber firing feels like pain rather than nothing or something else. The identity may be true, but it remains inexplicable — there is a gap between the physical description and the phenomenal reality. Frank Jackson's "Mary's Room" argument pressed this further: Mary, a color scientist confined to a black-and-white environment, knows all physical facts about color vision. When she first sees red, she learns something new. But if physicalism is true, she already knew everything there is to know. Therefore physicalism is false — there are non-physical facts about phenomenal experience. The responses are instructive about the options available. Type-B physicalism (Loar, Papineau): phenomenal concepts refer to physical states by a different route than theoretical concepts — the explanatory gap is an illusion produced by this conceptual difference, not evidence of ontological non-physicality. Illusionism (Frankish): phenomenal consciousness as traditionally understood is an illusion — introspection systematically misrepresents the character of mental states. These responses represent very different ways of living with a problem that refuses dissolution.

name: Material Constitution
description: The problem of material constitution asks whether two distinct things can be made of exactly the same matter in exactly the same place at the same time. A statue and the lump of clay it is made from appear to be numerically distinct objects — the statue can survive being reshaped (it would cease to be the statue) while the lump cannot (it would still exist as a differently shaped lump). Yet they seem to occupy the same spatial location and be composed of the same matter simultaneously. If they are numerically identical, we face the problem of "coincident objects" — two things with different persistence conditions cannot be identical. If they are numerically distinct, we face the problem of two objects in the same place at the same time, which seems to violate intuitions about spatial exclusion. Judith Jarvis Thomson and David Wiggins have proposed "constitution" as a relation distinct from identity: the lump constitutes the statue without being identical to it. Peter van Inwagen proposed eliminativism: only organisms and simples exist — statues, lumps, and tables are useful fictions. Four-dimensionalism offers another resolution: the statue and the lump are the same four-dimensional object but their three-dimensional "temporal parts" at any moment have different properties relative to their futures.

name: Contextualism and Relativism
description: Assessment-sensitive relativism, developed by John MacFarlane, holds that the truth of certain claims — about knowledge, taste, and moral matters — is not fixed by the context of utterance but is sensitive to the context of assessment: who is evaluating the claim and from what perspective. When I say "licorice is tasty," I am saying something that is true relative to my own taste standard but false relative to someone who hates licorice. Both assessments are correct from their respective perspectives. This differs from simple relativism (different people mean different things by "tasty") and from contextualism (the meaning of "tasty" shifts with the context of utterance). MacFarlane's view preserves the idea that there is a genuine disagreement between the licorice-lover and the licorice-hater — they are evaluating the same claim, not talking past each other — while allowing that both are making correct assessments from their own perspectives. The view has been extended to knowledge attributions: "I know that p" is true at the context of utterance but its truth is assessable relative to different epistemic standards. This preserves the intuition that skeptical challenges genuinely threaten knowledge claims while also explaining why we correctly claim knowledge in ordinary contexts. The philosophical cost: it requires a non-standard semantics in which propositions have truth values relative to contexts of assessment as well as contexts of utterance.

name: Extended Mind
description: The extended mind thesis, proposed by Andy Clark and David Chalmers in "The Extended Mind" (1998), holds that cognitive processes and mental states need not be confined to the skull and skin — they can extend into the environment when external resources play the right functional role. Otto, who has Alzheimer's disease, uses a notebook to store information he cannot reliably retain in biological memory. When he needs an address, he consults the notebook. Clark and Chalmers argue that the notebook plays exactly the functional role that biological memory plays in a neurotypical person — it is Otto's memory, not merely a tool he uses to access information. The "parity principle" grounds this claim: if a non-biological resource plays a functional role that we would unhesitatingly attribute to a cognitive process if it were inside the skull, then the resource counts as part of the cognitive system. The boundary between mind and world is determined by functional organization, not by the biological. The thesis connects to embodied cognition (cognition extends into the body) and enactivism (cognition extends into the environment through sensorimotor loops), forming what Clark calls the "extended, embodied, embedded, enactive" (4E) approach to cognition. Its implications for epistemology: if knowledge can be stored in extended cognitive systems, then the locus of epistemic evaluation shifts from individual biological agents to hybrid biological-technological systems.

name: Intellectual Virtues
description: Intellectual virtue epistemology identifies the traits and dispositions constituting epistemic excellence — what it is to be a good reasoner, a reliable knower, and a responsible inquirer. The intellectual virtues include: intellectual humility (accurate recognition of one's epistemic limitations), intellectual courage (willingness to consider difficult ideas and challenge orthodoxy), open-mindedness (genuine receptivity to new evidence and arguments), thoroughness (investigating questions with appropriate care), and intellectual honesty (forming and expressing beliefs in accordance with evidence rather than self-interest). Linda Zagzebski grounded these virtues in the same structure as moral virtues: they are stable character traits acquired through practice and education, consisting of a reliable disposition to produce excellent epistemic performance together with the appropriate motivational component — genuine love of truth and intellectual excellence. They are not merely reliable faculties but features of character for which agents can be held responsible. Jason Baehr connected intellectual virtue to democratic epistemology: a flourishing epistemic community requires citizens who practice intellectual virtues publicly — who are genuinely open to changing their minds, who engage seriously with opposing views, and who prioritize truth over tribal allegiance. Intellectual vice — epistemic cowardice, closed-mindedness, intellectual dishonesty — has social and political consequences beyond individual error.

name: Higher-Order Evidence
description: Higher-order evidence is evidence about the reliability of one's own reasoning processes or the quality of one's own beliefs — "meta-evidence" that can rationally require revision of first-order beliefs even when the first-order evidence has not changed. The most vivid source of higher-order evidence is peer disagreement: if I discover that an epistemic peer — someone equally competent and equally informed — reaches a different conclusion from the same evidence, this is evidence that one of us has made a reasoning error. How much should this move my credence? David Christensen and Adam Elga defend "conciliationism": disagreement with a genuine epistemic peer should significantly move you toward their position — you should split the difference between your credence and theirs, because you have no particular reason to trust your reasoning over theirs. Roger White defends "steadfastness": you are permitted (or even required) to maintain your original credence because your first-order evidence remains unchanged and peer disagreement provides no additional first-order evidence about the truth. The debate connects to larger questions about self-trust and epistemic autonomy: if conciliationism is correct and all peer disagreement should move us toward the mean, intellectual inquiry threatens to collapse into consensus. But if steadfastness is too permissive, it licenses dogmatic maintenance of any position in the face of persistent reasonable disagreement.

name: Belief Revision
description: Belief revision theory, developed by Carlos Alchourron, Peter Gärdenfors, and David Makinson (AGM theory), provides a formal framework for how a rational agent should modify their beliefs when new information arrives that may conflict with existing beliefs. If you believe the building is open on weekends and then learn it is closed on Sundays, how should your belief system be revised to accommodate this new information while preserving coherence? The AGM framework identifies three types of change: expansion (adding new beliefs compatible with existing ones), contraction (removing a belief without adding its negation), and revision (incorporating a new belief that contradicts existing beliefs, requiring the retraction of old beliefs to maintain consistency). The "Ramsey test" connects belief revision to conditionals: I believe "if A then B" if I would believe B after revising my beliefs to include A. Belief revision theory has profound connections to philosophy of science (theory change), legal reasoning (updating beliefs given new evidence), and artificial intelligence (knowledge base management). Its philosophical significance: it provides a normative account of rational doxastic change that goes beyond Bayesian updating, which handles only updates by conditionalizing on new evidence rather than the full range of belief-revision scenarios that arise in actual reasoning.

name: Bayesian Epistemology
description: Bayesian epistemology holds that rational belief comes in degrees — "credences" — and that rational belief revision proceeds by conditionalizing on new evidence according to Bayes's theorem. If I have prior credence P(H) in a hypothesis H, and I receive evidence E, my posterior credence should be P(H|E) = P(H) × P(E|H) / P(E). This theorem tells me how much evidence E should increase or decrease my credence in H based on how likely H makes E. The Bayesian approach has several attractive features: it captures the intuition that some evidence is stronger than others (evidence that was very unlikely if the hypothesis is false raises credence more than expected evidence), it naturally handles probabilistic reasoning, and it makes explicit the role of prior beliefs in rational inference. Bruno de Finetti and Frank Ramsey grounded subjective probability in coherence conditions on degrees of belief — avoiding the "Dutch book" (a series of bets that guarantee loss). The framework faces several challenges: the problem of the priors (where do our starting credences come from, and are they rational?), the problem of old evidence (Bayes's theorem says evidence that has probability 1 given background beliefs cannot update hypotheses, but old evidence often does support theories), and the problem of logical omniscience (Bayesians must know all logical consequences of their beliefs, which idealized rational agents do not have).

name: Memory and Personal Identity
description: Memory is central to both epistemology and the metaphysics of personal identity, and the two are intertwined in philosophically productive ways. For Locke, personal identity just is psychological continuity through memory: I am the same person as the child who had a particular experience because I can remember that experience from the inside. Memory is not just a source of knowledge about the past — it is constitutive of the past self's continued existence in the present self. The epistemology of memory is more complex than simple storage and retrieval. Memories are reconstructive, not reproductive: we do not simply play back stored representations but actively rebuild memories from fragments, expectations, schemas, and current interpretive frameworks. This makes memory systematically unreliable in ways that matter both epistemically (eyewitness testimony is notoriously unreliable) and personally (our sense of who we are is partly constructed from unreliable memory). Sydney Shoemaker's analysis of "quasi-memory" clarified the logical structure: quasi-remembering an experience is having an apparent memory of it that appropriately causally traces back to the experience, without the requirement that the subject be identical to the person who had the original experience. This allows one to separate the causal condition from the identity condition in memory, which is needed to coherently discuss hypothetical cases of brain transplant and fission. Existentialism  (6 concepts in this volume)

name: Frege's Philosophy of Language
description: Gottlob Frege's distinction between sense (Sinn) and reference (Bedeutung) is one of the most influential contributions to the philosophy of language. The reference of a term is the object it picks out; the sense is the "mode of presentation" — the way the object is presented to the mind through the term. "The morning star" and "the evening star" have the same reference (Venus) but different senses — they present Venus under different descriptions. This explains why "the morning star is the evening star" is an informative discovery, not a trivial identity. Frege extended this to sentences: the reference of a sentence is its truth-value (the True or the False), while the sense is the thought it expresses. In propositional attitude contexts — "John believes that the morning star is visible" — the terms refer not to their usual references but to their senses (they undergo "oblique reference"). This is Frege's explanation of the opacity of propositional attitude contexts. Frege's logic was equally revolutionary: he invented quantifier-variable notation and thereby made it possible to express the logical structure of mathematical reasoning for the first time. His logicist program — reducing arithmetic to pure logic — failed due to Russell's paradox, but his logical innovations are the foundation of modern formal logic and the philosophy of language.

name: Ordinary Language Philosophy
description: Ordinary language philosophy, centered at Oxford in the 1950s and 60s, held that many philosophical problems arise from the misuse of language — from terms being lifted out of their ordinary contexts and asked to perform impossible philosophical tasks. The remedy is not the construction of formal ideal languages (logical positivism's approach) but careful attention to how words actually function in ordinary speech. Dissolving philosophical problems by attending to ordinary language is the primary philosophical task. J.L. Austin exemplified the method: in "Other Minds" and "Sense and Sensibilia," he showed that philosophical puzzles about perception and knowledge of other minds arise from misapplying the terms "know," "certain," "perceive," and "appear." These terms have perfectly determinate ordinary uses that generate no paradox; the puzzles arise when philosophers extract them from their contexts and treat them as if they had acontextual, absolute meanings. Gilbert Ryle's "The Concept of Mind" applied ordinary language analysis to philosophy of mind: Descartes's mind-body dualism is a "category mistake" — treating mental terms as if they referred to a second kind of causal mechanism rather than understanding them as dispositional terms that describe how persons behave and what dispositions they have. The "ghost in the machine" is a philosophical illusion generated by systematic misunderstanding of mental language.

name: Meaning and Truth Conditions
description: Donald Davidson proposed that a theory of meaning for a language should take the form of a Tarski-style truth theory: to know the meaning of a sentence is to know its truth conditions — the conditions under which it would be true. A compositional truth theory specifies the truth conditions of every sentence in the language as a function of the meanings (semantic values) of its parts and their mode of combination. "Snow is white" is true if and only if snow is white. This truth-conditional semantics has been enormously influential, but Michael Dummett challenged its foundational assumption: we cannot understand a sentence's truth conditions if those conditions might obtain or fail to obtain in circumstances beyond our capacity to verify or falsify. Truth-conditional semantics presupposes a verification-transcendent notion of truth that is philosophically suspect — we cannot grasp what it means for truth to "outrun" our capacity to recognize it. Dummett's "anti-realism" replaces truth conditions with assertibility conditions: the meaning of a statement is its conditions of warranted assertion — the evidence that would entitle us to assert it. This connects to intuitionism in mathematics (replacing truth with provability) and has radical consequences for logic (the law of excluded middle is not generally valid) and for metaphysics (realism about unverifiable states of affairs is untenable).

name: Metaphor
description: Metaphor is a linguistic phenomenon in which a word or phrase literally meaning one thing is used to characterize or illuminate something else: "Juliet is the sun," "argument is war," "time is a river." Aristotle treated metaphor as the application of a name that belongs to something else through an analogy or resemblance — an ornamental device that clever speakers use for effect. On this traditional view, metaphors are reducible to their literal paraphrases; they add rhetorical flourish but no cognitive content. George Lakoff and Mark Johnson's Metaphors We Live By (1980) proposed a radical revision: metaphor is not primarily a linguistic phenomenon but a conceptual one. Our ordinary conceptual system is fundamentally metaphorical. We understand abstract domains — time, argument, causation, morality, mind — through the structure of concrete bodily experience. "Argument is war" is not merely a decorative phrase; it structures how we actually think about argument — we defend positions, attack claims, and demolish opposing views. This "conceptual metaphor" theory has enormous implications: if abstract thought is grounded in sensorimotor experience through metaphorical mapping, then embodied cognition is not just a feature of perception but of all abstract reasoning. The nature, meaning, and truth of metaphor — whether metaphors have truth conditions, whether they can be paraphrased without loss — remains central to philosophy of language and cognitive linguistics.

name: Pragmatics
description: Pragmatics is the study of how context contributes to meaning — the aspects of utterance meaning that go beyond the semantic content of the words used. H.P. Grice's theory of "conversational implicature" is the foundational contribution: speakers communicate more than they literally say, and hearers routinely infer these additional meanings by assuming the speaker is observing maxims of cooperative conversation — be truthful (Quality), be informative (Quantity), be relevant (Relation), and be clear (Manner). If I ask you whether you have read Smith's new book and you reply "I don't usually read philosophy," you implicate that you have not read it, though you have not said so. The implicature arises because your response would otherwise violate the Relevance maxim — and since we assume you are being cooperative, we infer the implication needed to make your response relevant. Dan Sperber and Deirdre Wilson's "relevance theory" offers a more unified account: communication is governed by a single principle of relevance (getting the most cognitive effects for the least processing effort), from which the Gricean maxims can be derived. Relevance theory explains how hearers routinely interpret utterances far more specifically than their literal content requires — inferring intended reference, resolving ambiguities, and recovering unstated assumptions — through a single cognitive optimization process.

name: Philosophy of Fiction
description: Fiction poses distinctive puzzles for philosophy of language: what is the nature of fictional names ("Sherlock Holmes"), what is the logical status of fictional truths ("Holmes lived on Baker Street"), and how can we have genuine emotional responses to characters we know do not exist? Each puzzle challenges standard accounts of reference, truth, and emotion. Kendall Walton's "make-believe" theory is the most influential unified answer: fictional works are "props" in games of make-believe — like children's games where a stick is "really" a horse. When we engage with fiction, we imagine the prescribed scenarios as part of a sanctioned game. Our "emotions" toward fictional characters are quasi-emotions: they have the phenomenology of real emotions but are generated within the imaginative game rather than through belief in the existence of their objects. Gregory Currie's account emphasizes the role of simulation: we understand fictional characters by imaginatively placing ourselves in their situations and simulating their mental states. Fiction serves to expand our understanding of how minds work under circumstances we have not personally encountered. The philosophical stakes: if Walton is right, fiction is a species of play with no truth-apt content; if Currie is right, fiction is a cognitive technology for expanding the range of mental states we can model.

name: Logical Consequence
description: What does it mean for a conclusion to follow logically from premises? Tarski's model-theoretic account: an argument is valid if and only if there is no model — no interpretation of the non-logical terms — in which the premises are true and the conclusion false. Logical consequence is thus defined as truth-preservation across all interpretations, where logical constants (and, or, not, if-then, all, some) retain fixed meanings while non-logical vocabulary varies. This formal account captures the intuition that logical validity is independent of content — it does not matter what the non-logical terms mean, as long as the logical form preserves truth. But the account raises immediate questions: which terms count as "logical"? What distinguishes the logical constants from other terms? And can we give a non-circular characterization of the logical constants? JC Beall and Greg Restall have developed "logical pluralism": there are multiple correct accounts of logical consequence, each corresponding to a different way of specifying what it means to "preserve truth across cases." Classical logic, relevant logic, paraconsistent logic, and intuitionistic logic each capture a legitimate notion of logical consequence suited to different applications. This pluralism is philosophically controversial — many philosophers hold that there is exactly one correct account of logical consequence, and the task of logic is to identify it. Political Philosophy  (7 concepts in this volume)

name: Recognition and Misrecognition
description: The politics of recognition, developed by Axel Honneth drawing on Hegel's master-slave dialectic, holds that self-identity and self-realization require mutual recognition — the acknowledgment by others of one's worth, dignity, and particularity. Misrecognition — being systematically denied recognition, having one's identity distorted or denigrated by dominant cultural representations — is a form of injustice that damages the self and generates legitimate demands for political remedy. Honneth identified three spheres of recognition: love (recognition of the whole person in intimate relationships), law (recognition as a rights-bearing subject), and solidarity (recognition of one's distinctive contribution to shared social values). Denials of recognition in each sphere — abuse, legal exclusion, cultural denigration — produce distinctive pathologies: damaged self-confidence, lost self-respect, and diminished self-esteem. Nancy Fraser challenged the primacy of recognition in justice theory: some political struggles are primarily about redistribution of economic resources, not recognition of cultural identity, and the "recognition paradigm" risks displacing economic critique with cultural politics. Her response: a "bivalent" theory of justice that treats redistribution and recognition as co-primary and irreducible dimensions, resisting the tendency to subordinate one to the other.

name: Political Obligation
description: Political obligation asks whether and why citizens are morally bound to obey the laws of their state — not just prudentially (to avoid punishment) but genuinely morally obligated. This question is distinct from political legitimacy (whether a government has the right to rule) and from moral permissibility (whether specific laws are morally acceptable). It concerns the general duty of compliance with law. Social contract theories ground political obligation in consent: we are obligated to obey because we have (actually or hypothetically) agreed to be governed. The persistent objection: no one has actually consented to be governed, and the moves to "tacit consent" (remaining in a territory) or "hypothetical consent" (what rational agents would agree to) stretch the concept of consent beyond recognition. T.H. Green's "social membership" theory grounds political obligation in the value of social membership itself: the state maintains the conditions for the realization of our capacities as social beings, and this generates genuine obligations of support and compliance. Fair play theories (Hart, Rawls): citizens who benefit from a cooperative scheme governed by law are obligated to do their fair share by complying with the scheme. The debate about whether political obligation exists, and on what grounds, remains one of political philosophy's most genuinely open questions.

name: Race and Political Philosophy
description: Charles Mills's The Racial Contract (1997) argues that the social contract tradition in political philosophy has been systematically racially exclusive: the canonical contract theorists produced theories that, in their historical context, applied only to white Europeans. The "Racial Contract" is the actual normative contract that structured European colonial societies — an agreement among whites to subordinate non-whites and to misrepresent this subordination as consistent with the universal principles the official social contract proclaims. W.E.B. Du Bois's concept of "double consciousness" — always seeing oneself through the eyes of a society that regards one as inferior — provides the phenomenological complement to Mills's structural analysis. Racial oppression is not only a matter of economic exploitation and legal exclusion but of psychological damage, the distortion of self-understanding under the sustained gaze of white supremacy. The implications for political philosophy: a political philosophy adequate to actually existing societies must take structural racism seriously as a fundamental feature of those societies rather than treating it as an unfortunate deviation from liberal principles. This requires not merely extending the canonical theories to include everyone but reconsidering the theories themselves in light of the structural conditions they have historically ignored.

name: Utopianism
description: Utopianism in political philosophy is the tradition of imagining radically better societies — societies that fully realize principles of justice, equality, freedom, or human flourishing that existing societies fall far short of. Thomas More's Utopia (1516) gave the tradition its name: an island society organized on principles of common property, religious tolerance, and rational governance, deliberately contrasted with the injustices of Tudor England. Ernst Bloch's The Principle of Hope (1954–59) rehabilitated utopian thinking against Marxist dismissals of "utopian socialism": utopian imagination is not escapist fantasy but the expression of the "not-yet" — the unrealized potential present in every historical moment. Philosophy's task is to bring this latent possibility to articulate consciousness, enabling it to become a real force for transformation. Critics of utopianism — from Burke to Popper to Gray — argue that the pursuit of perfect societies leads to terror: the gap between utopian ideals and actually existing human beings can only be closed by force, producing the totalitarian projects of the twentieth century. The "blueprint utopias" of communism and fascism are the nightmare versions of the utopian imagination. Against this, defenders argue that the capacity to imagine radically better arrangements is a precondition for any genuine political transformation. Mind & Consciousness  (8 concepts in this volume)

name: Neo-Confucianism
description: Neo-Confucianism, flourishing from the Song dynasty (960–1279 CE) onward, revitalized Confucian ethics and metaphysics by engaging seriously with Buddhist and Daoist concepts while maintaining the Confucian commitment to social ethics and human cultivation. The movement's philosophical achievement was to provide Confucian ethics with a metaphysical foundation it had previously lacked. Zhu Xi (1130–1200 CE), the great systematizer, developed a metaphysics built on the interplay of li (principle/pattern) and qi (vital energy/matter). Li is the eternal rational principle that gives everything its nature and its normative standard; qi is the energetic stuff through which li is actualized in particular things. The investigation of things (gewu) — the careful study of principles in things and affairs — is the path to moral knowledge. Wang Yangming (1472–1529 CE) challenged Zhu Xi's externalism: the principles are not in things but in the mind. "Knowing and acting are one" — genuine moral knowledge is inseparable from moral action. The project of investigating principles in external things mistakes the location of moral truth. Wang's "philosophy of mind" (xinxue) grounds ethics in the innate moral knowledge (liangzhi) present in every mind, accessible through sincere introspection and cultivation rather than external investigation.

name: Taoism and Nature
description: Taoism's philosophy of nature holds that the natural world is the primary manifestation of the Tao — the fundamental principle of spontaneous, self-organizing order. Nature is not a resource to be dominated or a backdrop for human action but the most transparent expression of the way things naturally unfold when not distorted by human interference, artificiality, or desire. The natural cycles of growth and decay, the transformations of the seasons, and the adaptive responses of living things to their environments all embody the Tao in action. Zhuangzi's nature philosophy goes furthest: the divisions human beings draw between self and other, between life and death, between beautiful and ugly, are conventional constructions imposed on a reality that flows continuously through all these apparent opposites. The "Perfect Man" (zhiren) of Zhuangzi rides the changes of nature without resistance — not passively but with total responsiveness, transforming with the Tao rather than opposing it. The ecological implications of Taoism have made it a resource for contemporary environmental philosophy: its critique of the drive to master and exploit nature, its emphasis on harmony with natural processes rather than conquest of them, and its view that human beings are participants in a natural order rather than its appointed managers have all been developed by contemporary environmental thinkers seeking alternatives to the dominant Western framework of nature as resource.

name: Buddhist Epistemology
description: Buddhist epistemology, developed primarily by Dignaga (fifth century CE) and Dharmakirti (seventh century CE), is a rigorous philosophical system that engages directly with the Nyaya tradition while defending distinctively Buddhist metaphysical commitments. Dignaga's Pramanasamuccaya ("Compendium of Valid Cognition") restricted valid knowledge to two sources: perception (pratyaksha) and inference (anumana). All other claimed sources — comparison, testimony, postulation — reduce to these two or are invalid. Dignaga's account of perception is particularly sophisticated: genuine perception is free from all conceptual construction (kalpana) — it grasps only the bare, particular, unconceptualized "suchness" of a thing. The moment any concept is applied, we have moved from pure perception into interpretation. This has the striking consequence that perceptual knowledge is knowledge of bare particulars, and all the rich structured world of our ordinary experience is a conceptual construction built on this minimal perceptual base. Dharmakirti extended Dignaga's system and developed rigorous arguments for key Buddhist metaphysical claims — including the momentariness of all phenomena (everything exists for only an instant) and the ultimately mental character of causation. His engagement with Nyaya and Mimamsa opponents produced some of the most technically demanding philosophical debate in any tradition, anticipating many moves in contemporary Western epistemology.

name: Creativity and Genius
description: The philosophy of creativity asks what makes artistic production genuinely creative — what distinguishes the work of genius from competent execution, and whether creativity involves a distinctive cognitive or psychological mode. Kant's account of artistic genius in the Critique of Judgment is central: genius is the "talent (natural gift) that gives the rule to art." The genius cannot be taught or explain the rules they follow; their productive imagination generates work that becomes exemplary for others — a rule that can be followed but not articulated in advance. Schopenhauer connected artistic genius to the capacity for sustained "will-less" contemplation: the genius is one who can step outside the ordinary orientation toward objects as potential satisfiers of will and perceive them as pure Platonic Forms — as universal patterns of existence accessible through their particular instantiations. This capacity for detached, sustained attention to aesthetic form is the mark of genius and explains why artistic insight can illuminate universal human experience. Contemporary accounts engage with the "creative process" empirically: the role of preparation, incubation, insight, and verification in problem-solving and artistic production. The "10,000 hours" account of expertise challenges Romantic notions of inexplicable genius by reducing creativity to mastery of a domain's structures. The philosophical tension: can the genuinely novel — the truly unprecedented — be explained by practice and accumulation, or does creativity require something that cannot be fully explained by prior causes?

name: Aesthetic Value
description: Aesthetic value — the distinctive kind of goodness that works of art and natural beauty possess — raises questions about its nature, its grounds, and whether it is objective or subjective. Hume's "standard of taste" represents the classic response to the apparent subjectivity of aesthetic judgment: while taste varies among individuals, some judges are better than others — those with greater experience, finer discrimination, freedom from prejudice, and the capacity for comparison. The standard of taste is not fixed by one person's judgment but by the convergence of ideally qualified judges.
